title: Nature & Scope of Audit Engagements
description: Understand the different types of engagements CPAs perform and the assurance spectrum

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Understanding the different types of engagements CPAs perform is FUNDAMENTAL to AUD. The exam tests your ability to distinguish between audits, reviews, compilations, and other services—and to know what level of assurance each provides!

sections.title: The Assurance Spectrum
sections.content:
**Assurance = Confidence in the reliability of information**

**Levels of assurance from highest to lowest:**

1. **Audit (Examination):** Reasonable assurance (HIGH)
2. **Review:** Limited assurance (MODERATE)
3. **Compilation/Preparation:** No assurance (NONE)
4. **Agreed-Upon Procedures:** No opinion—just report findings

**Key concept:** More assurance = More work = More cost

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Assurance Levels
sections.content:
**"ARLC" = Audit, Review, Limited procedures, Compilation**

**Think of it as a staircase:**
🔝 AUDIT - "I'm POSITIVE this is fairly stated" (reasonable)
📊 REVIEW - "Nothing came to my attention" (limited)
📝 COMPILATION - "I just organized their numbers" (none)

**Reasonable ≠ Absolute!** Even audits provide REASONABLE, not absolute, assurance.

sections.title: What is an Audit?
sections.content:
**Definition:** Systematic process of objectively obtaining and evaluating evidence about assertions to form an opinion.

**Purpose:** Express an opinion on whether financial statements are fairly presented in accordance with the applicable framework (GAAP, IFRS, etc.)

**Key characteristics:**
• Independence REQUIRED
• Tests of controls and substantive procedures
• Reasonable (not absolute) assurance
• Expresses OPINION

sections.title: Reasonable Assurance Explained
sections.content:
**Why not absolute assurance?**

**Inherent limitations of an audit:**
• Sampling (can't test everything)
• Judgment involved in selecting procedures
• Management representations required
• Persuasive, not conclusive, evidence
• Concealment and collusion by management
• Internal control limitations

**Reasonable assurance = HIGH level of assurance**
(but acknowledges limitations exist)

sections.title: Review vs Audit
sections.content:
**Review engagements:**
• LIMITED assurance
• Primarily inquiry and analytical procedures
• NO tests of internal controls
• NO detailed substantive testing
• CONCLUSION (not opinion)
• Less expensive than audit

**Wording difference:**
• Audit: "In our OPINION, the F/S are fairly presented..."
• Review: "We are not aware of any material modifications..."

sections.title: Compilation and Preparation
sections.content:
**Compilation:**
• Present management's financial data in F/S format
• NO assurance provided
• Report states no assurance given
• Independence NOT required (but must disclose if not independent)

**Preparation:**
• Prepare F/S using client records
• NO assurance
• NO report required
• Must include "no assurance" legend on each page

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Standards Framework
sections.content:
**Know which standards apply:**

**GAAS (SAS) - Generally Accepted Auditing Standards:**
• Financial statement AUDITS of nonissuers

**PCAOB Standards:**
• Audits of ISSUERS (public companies)

**SSARS - Statements on Standards for Accounting and Review Services:**
• Compilations, Reviews, Preparations

**SSAE - Statements on Standards for Attestation Engagements:**
• Examination, Review, AUP of subject matter OTHER than F/S

sections.title: Issuer vs Nonissuer
sections.content:
**Issuer (Public Company):**
• Registered with SEC
• Subject to PCAOB standards
• Integrated audit (F/S + ICFR)
• Critical Audit Matters (CAMs) required

**Nonissuer (Private Company):**
• Not SEC registered
• Subject to AICPA standards (GAAS)
• ICFR audit optional
• Key Audit Matters (KAMs) optional
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Assurance Levels: What Your Boss *Really* Means
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. 'Reasonable assurance' in an audit doesn't mean we guarantee the financial statements are perfect. Think of it like this: we're saying, 'We looked *really* hard, kicked the tires, and didn't find anything materially misstated.' 'Limited assurance' in a review is more like, 'We asked a few questions, glanced around, and nothing screamed at us.' And 'no assurance'? Well, that's pretty much what it sounds like. Don't expect a pat on the back for finding a needle in a haystack when you're only using a magnet the size of a quarter.

sections.title: Mixing Up Reviews & Compilations: Avoid This Pitfall
sections.content: A frequent error is confusing a review engagement with a compilation/preparation. Students think, 'They both have less assurance than an audit, so they must be similar.' Wrong! A review requires analytical procedures and inquiries to provide limited assurance. A compilation/preparation simply presents financial data without expressing any opinion. Imagine preparing tax returns: you're taking the client's information and putting it in the right boxes. That's similar to a compilation. A review, however, is about asking questions and performing analysis to assess whether the information makes sense.

sections.title: Sarah's First Agreed-Upon Procedures Nightmare
sections.content: Sarah, a bright-eyed staff auditor, was assigned an agreed-upon procedures engagement to verify a company's inventory count. She meticulously followed the procedures, but the client later complained that she didn't identify a significant obsolescence issue. The lesson? Agreed-upon procedures are limited to *specifically* what you're asked to do; you don't have a general obligation to sniff out other problems beyond the agreed scope. Sarah should have clarified the scope with the client beforehand to avoid misunderstandings and potential liability.

sections.title: Choosing the Right Engagement: Real-World Impact
sections.content: Selecting the appropriate engagement type is crucial for client satisfaction and managing risk. Imagine a small business seeking a loan. A compilation might be sufficient for internal use, but the bank will likely demand a review or even an audit. Offering the *wrong* service can jeopardize the client's financing, damage your firm's reputation, and open you up to liability. Understanding the assurance spectrum isn't just textbook knowledge; it's the foundation of your professional judgment and ability to serve your clients effectively. This is a daily decision for CPAs.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Audit provides REASONABLE assurance; review provides LIMITED assurance
Compilation and preparation provide NO assurance
GAAS/SAS for nonissuer audits; PCAOB for issuer audits
SSARS for compilations, reviews, preparations
SSAE for attestation engagements on subject matter other than F/S
Reasonable assurance acknowledges inherent audit limitations
Independence required for audits and reviews; disclosed if lacking for compilations

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following engagements provides the highest level of assurance?
options: Agreed-Upon Procedures
Review
Audit
Compilation
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An audit (examination) provides reasonable assurance, which is the highest level of assurance among the options. A review provides limited assurance, while agreed-upon procedures and compilations provide no assurance.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the level of investigation and evidence gathering involved in each type of engagement.

reveal.question: What is the definition of an audit?
reveal.answer: An audit is a systematic process of objectively obtaining and evaluating evidence about assertions to form an opinion about whether financial statements are fairly presented in accordance with the applicable framework (e.g., GAAP, IFRS).

rows.label: Level of Assurance
values: Reasonable (High)
Limited (Moderate)
None

rows.label: Opinion Expressed
values: Yes
Yes, with limited assurance
No

rows.label: Procedures Performed
values: Extensive; includes testing of internal controls and substantive procedures
Primarily inquiry and analytical procedures
Primarily compiling financial information based on client data

title: AICPA Code of Professional Conduct
description: Master the ethical principles and rules governing CPA conduct

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The AICPA Code of Professional Conduct is the ethical foundation of the CPA profession. Understanding these principles and rules—especially independence, integrity, and objectivity—is heavily tested on FAR and essential for your career!

sections.title: Structure of the Code
sections.content:
**The Code consists of:**

**1. Principles:** Aspirational goals (6 principles)
**2. Rules:** Enforceable standards
**3. Interpretations:** Guidance on applying rules
**4. Other Guidance:** Nonauthoritative help

**Organized by member type:**
• Members in Public Practice
• Members in Business
• Other Members

sections.title: Six Principles of Professional Conduct
rows: Exercise sensitive professional and moral judgments
Act in a way that serves the public interest
Maintain objectivity; be independent when required
Observe Codes principles in determining scope of services

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The 6 Principles
sections.content:
**"RIPODS"** (like iPods but for ethics!)

**R**esponsibilities
**I**ntegrity
**P**ublic interest
**O**bjectivity & independence
**D**ue care
**S**cope & nature of services

**Or think:** A CPA's reputation depends on these 6 pillars!

sections.title: Integrity
sections.content:
**Definition:** Being honest and candid within constraints of client confidentiality

**Key requirements:**
• Don't subordinate judgment to others
• Don't knowingly misrepresent facts
• Don't allow bias or conflict of interest

**Integrity breach examples:**
• Signing off on false financial statements
• Omitting material information
• Misrepresenting qualifications

sections.title: Objectivity and Independence
sections.content:
**Objectivity:** Freedom from conflicts of interest

**Independence:**
• Required for ATTEST services (audit, review, examination)
• Two aspects:
  - Independence in FACT (state of mind)
  - Independence in APPEARANCE (perception)

**Not required for:** Compilations, tax services, consulting (though objectivity still required)

sections.title: General Standards
sections.content:
**All members must:**

**1. Professional Competence:**
• Undertake only services you can complete competently
• Maintain competence through CPE

**2. Due Professional Care:**
• Exercise appropriate care and diligence
• Adequately plan and supervise

**3. Planning and Supervision:**
• Adequately plan and supervise work

**4. Sufficient Relevant Data:**
• Obtain sufficient data to support conclusions

sections.title: Confidentiality
sections.content:
**Rule:** Don't disclose confidential client information without consent

**Exceptions (may disclose):**
• Compliance with GAAS (quality review)
• Response to valid subpoena/summons
• Ethics investigation (peer review, state board)
• State laws requiring disclosure
• Client consent

**No privilege:** Unlike attorneys, CPA-client communications are NOT privileged at federal level

sections.title: Acts Discreditable
sections.content:
**Prohibited acts include:**

• Discrimination or harassment
• Negligence in preparing F/S or records
• Failure to file tax returns or pay taxes
• Failure to follow government audit standards
• Disclosing CPA exam questions/answers
• Solicitation using coercion or false advertising

**Consequence:** Disciplinary action by AICPA and/or state board

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Contingent Fees
sections.content:
**Contingent fees are PROHIBITED for:**
• Audits and reviews of F/S
• Compilations when third party will use and not disclose independence
• Examination of prospective F/S
• Original tax return preparation

**Contingent fees ALLOWED for:**
• Tax representation (audits, appeals)
• Amended returns (refund claims)
• Non-attest consulting services

sections.title: Advertising and Solicitation
sections.content:
**Permitted:**
• Advertising (must not be false, misleading, or deceptive)
• Solicitation of new clients
• Listing areas of practice

**Prohibited:**
• False or misleading advertising
• Coercion, overreaching, harassment
• Claiming superiority that can't be substantiated

sections.title: Form of Organization
sections.content:
**CPAs may practice in various forms:**
• Sole proprietorship
• Partnership
• Professional corporation
• LLC/LLP

**Requirements:**
• Majority ownership by CPAs
• Non-CPA owners must meet competency requirements
• CPA must have ultimate responsibility for services
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Independence: It's More Than Just a Feeling
sections.content: Okay, so the AICPA Code sounds dry, right? But trust me, independence is the bedrock of our profession. I've seen firms lose huge clients (and face lawsuits) because they got too cozy. Think about it: would *you* trust an audit if the auditor was best friends with the CFO? The Code's rules aren't just suggestions; they're there to protect the public's trust. So, memorize those rules, internalize them, and remember, a little distance goes a long way. Don't let 'em buy you lunch, people!

sections.title: Confusing Principles and Rules: A Costly Error
sections.content: Students often mix up the AICPA Code's Principles (aspirational goals) with its Rules (enforceable standards). It's a critical distinction! For example, 'Integrity' is a principle – we should all strive to be honest. But a specific rule might prohibit accepting gifts that impair independence. Failing to meet a Principle *alone* isn't a violation. However, violating a Rule *is*. Always remember: Rules are the 'musts,' Principles are the 'shoulds.' Know the difference, or you'll be in for a rude awakening on exam day (and in your career!).

sections.title: The Gift Basket Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a first-year auditor, received a lavish gift basket from a client after completing a successful inventory observation. The basket included gourmet chocolates and a high-end bottle of wine. Sarah, unsure of the firm's policy, accepted the basket, thinking it was a nice gesture. The learning point? Even seemingly innocent gifts can impair independence, or at least *appear* to. Sarah should have consulted the firm's policy and likely declined the gift, or reported it to a supervisor for guidance, to avoid any appearance of impropriety.

sections.title: Navigating Ethical Dilemmas in Real Life
sections.content: In practice, you'll constantly face ethical gray areas. Clients will pressure you to cut corners, bend the rules, or overlook minor issues. Understanding the AICPA Code isn't just about passing the exam; it's about knowing how to handle these situations. For example, imagine a client asks you to backdate a document. Knowing the 'Integrity and Objectivity' rule will give you the courage to stand your ground and refuse. This ethical foundation will protect you, your firm, and the public interest throughout your career.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Six principles: Responsibilities, Integrity, Public interest, Objectivity, Due care, Scope
Independence required for attest services; objectivity always required
Independence has two aspects: in fact AND in appearance
Confidentiality exceptions: subpoena, ethics investigation, quality review, client consent
Contingent fees prohibited for audits, reviews, most tax returns
Acts discreditable: discrimination, negligence, failure to file/pay taxes
CPA-client communications are NOT privileged at federal level

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following best describes the enforceability of the Principles section of the AICPA Code of Professional Conduct?
options: The Principles are enforceable standards and a violation can lead to disciplinary action.
The Principles are aspirational goals and are not enforceable standards.
The Principles are enforceable only for members in public practice.
The Principles are only used as guidance when interpreting the Rules of Conduct.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Principles section of the AICPA Code of Professional Conduct outlines aspirational goals for CPAs and are not, in themselves, enforceable. The Rules of Conduct are the enforceable standards.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the difference between what you *should* do and what you *must* do.

reveal.question: Define 'Integrity' as it is used within the AICPA Code of Professional Conduct.
reveal.answer: Integrity means being honest and candid within the constraints of client confidentiality. It includes not subordinating judgment to others, not knowingly misrepresenting facts, and avoiding bias or conflicts of interest.

knowledgeCheck.question: A CPA in business is pressured by their supervisor to record revenue prematurely to meet quarterly targets. The CPA believes this would materially misstate the financial statements. According to the Integrity principle of the AICPA Code of Professional Conduct, what is the CPA's most appropriate course of action?
options: Comply with the supervisor's request to maintain their employment.
Discuss the matter with the company's external auditors.
Document their concerns and comply with the supervisor's request.
Refuse to comply with the supervisor's request, even if it means potential job loss.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The Integrity principle requires CPAs to not subordinate their judgment to others. Even if it means potential job loss, the CPA must refuse to comply with a request that would result in materially misstated financial statements.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the potential consequences of misrepresenting financial information.

title: Independence: Conceptual Framework
description: Apply the threats and safeguards approach to evaluate auditor independence

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Independence is the CORNERSTONE of audit credibility. Without independence, an auditor's opinion is worthless! The conceptual framework approach helps you identify threats to independence and apply safeguards—a skill tested repeatedly on AUD.

sections.title: Two Aspects of Independence
sections.content:
**Independence in FACT:**
• State of mind
• Unbiased mental attitude
• Objectivity in forming opinions
• Can't be directly observed

**Independence in APPEARANCE:**
• How others perceive the auditor
• Would a reasonable observer conclude auditor is independent?
• Avoiding relationships that APPEAR to impair objectivity

**Both are required!**

sections.title: Conceptual Framework Approach
sections.content:
**Three-step process:**

**Step 1: Identify threats** to compliance with independence rules

**Step 2: Evaluate significance** of threats (individually and combined)

**Step 3: Apply safeguards** when necessary to reduce threats to acceptable level

**If threats can't be reduced:** Don't provide the service or decline the engagement

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: The 5 Threats
sections.content:
**"SASSY"** threats to independence:

**S**elf-Interest (money in my pocket)
**A**dvocacy (championing their cause)
**S**elf-Review (checking my own work)
**S**ocializing/Familiarity (too friendly)
**Y**ielding to pressure (Undue influence)

**Think:** An auditor who's SASSY about threats will lose independence!

sections.title: Self-Interest Threat Examples
sections.content:
**Financial relationships:**
• Direct financial interest in client (ANY amount)
• Material indirect financial interest
• Loans to/from client
• Close relative with financial interest

**Business relationships:**
• Employment negotiations with client
• Overdue fees from client
• Contingent fees
• Significant fee dependence on one client

sections.title: Self-Review Threat Examples
sections.content:
**Performing services you later audit:**
• Designing internal controls
• Preparing F/S or accounting records
• Performing valuation services
• Implementing IT systems
• Bookkeeping services

**Key:** If you created it, you can't objectively evaluate it!

sections.title: Familiarity Threat Examples
sections.content:
**Close relationships:**
• Long association with client (partner rotation required)
• Family members in key client positions
• Former firm employee joins client
• Accepting gifts/hospitality
• Social relationships with client management

**Danger:** Being too close can cloud judgment

sections.title: Advocacy and Undue Influence
sections.content:
**Advocacy threats:**
• Acting as promoter of client securities
• Being expert witness supporting client's position
• Negotiating on client's behalf

**Undue Influence threats:**
• Actual or threatened litigation
• Pressure to reduce scope
• Pressure to accept inappropriate accounting
• Threats to replace auditor

sections.title: Safeguards
sections.content:
**Types of safeguards:**

**Created by profession/legislation:**
• Education and experience requirements
• CPE requirements
• Corporate governance regulations
• External review (peer review)

**Within the firm:**
• Quality control policies
• Partner rotation
• Separate engagement quality review
• Consultation requirements

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Direct vs Indirect Financial Interest
sections.content:
**DIRECT financial interest: ALWAYS impairs independence**
• Owning client stock directly
• Even $1 of stock = impaired!

**INDIRECT financial interest: Only if MATERIAL**
• Mutual fund that owns client stock
• Retirement plan with client investment
• Evaluate materiality to the CPA

**Key:** Direct = no threshold; Indirect = materiality matters

sections.title: Covered Members
sections.content:
**Who must be independent?**

**Covered members include:**
• Individual on attest engagement team
• Individual able to influence engagement
• Partner in office providing engagement
• Firm and its employee benefit plans
• Entity controlled by any of above

**Immediate family:** Spouse, spousal equivalent, dependent

**Close relatives:** Parents, siblings, nondependent children
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Independence: It's All About Perception, Folks!
sections.content: Let's be honest, independence in fact is great, but nobody can read your mind! The PCAOB doesn't care if *you* think you're unbiased. They care what a reasonable person would think. Did you just inherit a ton of stock in your audit client? Even if you swear you'll be objective, it LOOKS bad. It's like dating your boss's kid – awkward, potentially disastrous, and definitely a perception problem. So, think about how things appear, not just how they are.

sections.title: Threats vs. Safeguards: Don't Mix Them Up!
sections.content: Students often confuse identifying a threat with applying a safeguard. A threat is the *risk* to independence (e.g., a financial interest in the client). A safeguard is the *action* taken to mitigate that risk (e.g., selling the stock). Don't say 'The safeguard is that I own stock in the client.' That's the threat! The safeguard is what you do to fix it. Think of it like a disease (threat) and a cure (safeguard). Identify the disease first, then find the cure.

sections.title: The Country Club Membership Conundrum
sections.content: John, a partner at a small firm, enjoys playing golf. He recently received an invitation to join the same exclusive country club as the CFO of his largest audit client, at a heavily discounted rate offered only to valued business partners. The learning point here is that even seemingly innocent perks can create a self-interest threat to independence, especially if the discount is substantial and not available to the general public. John needs to carefully evaluate the significance of this threat and consider safeguards.

sections.title: Independence Checks: A Daily Reality
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter independence checks constantly. Before onboarding a new client, accepting a gift, or even having dinner with a client's employee, you need to run it by your firm's independence team. Firms use software and databases to track potential conflicts. This isn't just a formality; it's your responsibility to ensure your actions don't compromise the firm's independence, and potentially your own career. Neglecting this could result in fines, sanctions, or even loss of your CPA license.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Independence has two aspects: in fact AND in appearance
Three-step conceptual framework: Identify threats → Evaluate → Apply safeguards
Five threat categories: Self-interest, Advocacy, Self-review, Familiarity, Undue influence
Direct financial interest ALWAYS impairs independence (no materiality threshold)
Indirect financial interest impairs only if material to the CPA
Covered members include engagement team, partners in office, and firm
If safeguards can't reduce threats → Don't perform the service

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following best describes 'Independence in Appearance'?
options: The auditor's unbiased mental attitude and objectivity.
The auditor's actual lack of bias, even if unknown to others.
How a reasonable observer would perceive the auditor's objectivity.
A state of mind that allows the auditor to perform an attest service without being affected by influences.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Independence in Appearance focuses on how others perceive the auditor's objectivity. Even if the auditor is truly independent (Independence in Fact), if a reasonable observer would conclude otherwise, independence is impaired.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the word 'appearance' - what does it imply?

reveal.question: List the three steps of the Conceptual Framework approach to independence.
reveal.answer:
1. Identify threats to compliance with independence rules.
2. Evaluate the significance of the threats (individually and combined).
3. Apply safeguards when necessary to reduce threats to an acceptable level. If threats cannot be reduced, independence is impaired, and the service should not be provided.

rows.label: Definition
values: Auditor's state of mind; Unbiased mental attitude
How others perceive the auditor's independence

rows.label: Observability
values: Cannot be directly observed
Can be assessed by a reasonable observer

rows.label: Focus
values: Objectivity in forming opinions
Avoiding relationships that appear to compromise objectivity

title: SEC & PCAOB Independence Rules
description: Understand the stricter independence requirements for public company audits

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: When auditing PUBLIC companies (issuers), stricter SEC and PCAOB rules apply—not just AICPA rules! After Enron, Sarbanes-Oxley created additional independence requirements. These differences are frequently tested on AUD!

sections.title: Regulatory Framework
sections.content:
**For Issuer (Public Company) Audits:**

**SEC:** Sets independence rules for auditors of SEC registrants

**PCAOB:** Enforces standards for registered accounting firms

**Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX):** Congressional law establishing requirements

**Note:** PCAOB and SEC rules are generally MORE RESTRICTIVE than AICPA rules

sections.title: Prohibited Non-Audit Services
sections.content:
**Under SOX, auditors of issuers CANNOT provide:**

1. Bookkeeping and related services
2. Financial information systems design/implementation
3. Appraisal or valuation services
4. Actuarial services
5. Internal audit outsourcing
6. Management functions or human resources
7. Broker-dealer, investment advisor, or investment banking
8. Legal services unrelated to audit
9. Expert services for legal proceedings

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Prohibited Services
sections.content:
**"BAFTA-HELM"** (like the British film awards steering a ship!)

**B**ookkeeping
**A**ppraisal/valuation
**F**inancial systems design
**T**ax services (aggressive planning - separate rule)
**A**ctuarial services

**H**uman resources
**E**xpert witness (legal)
**L**egal services
**M**anagement functions

**Think:** These services make auditor captain of client's ship = not independent!

sections.title: Tax Services Restrictions
sections.content:
**Tax services generally ALLOWED for issuers, BUT:**

**Prohibited:**
• Marketing aggressive tax shelters
• Tax services for certain executives (CEO, CFO, CAO, controller)
• Confidential or aggressive tax transactions

**Required:**
• Pre-approval by audit committee
• Disclosure of fees paid

**Key:** Tax compliance generally OK; aggressive planning NOT OK

sections.title: Audit Committee Pre-Approval
sections.content:
**ALL audit and non-audit services must be:**

**Pre-approved by audit committee** (or designated member)

**Includes:**
• Audit services
• Audit-related services
• Permitted tax services
• Any other allowed non-audit services

**De minimis exception:** Up to 5% of total fees if:
• Services not recognized as non-audit at start
• Promptly brought to committee's attention
• Approved before audit completion

sections.title: Employment Cooling-Off Period
sections.content:
**One-year cooling-off period required if:**

Former engagement team member joins issuer client in:
• CEO, CFO, CAO, Controller position
• Financial reporting oversight role
• Any equivalent position

**The firm is NOT independent** during this period!

**Applies to:** Members of engagement team who provided 10+ hours of services in 12 months before employment

sections.title: Financial Relationships
sections.content:
**SEC rules for financial interests:**

**Firm cannot have financial interest in:**
• Audit client
• Client's parent, subsidiaries, affiliates

**Covered persons:**
• Firm partners
• Managers who supervise audit
• All partners in office participating in audit
• Anyone in chain of command

**More expansive than AICPA "covered member" concept**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: AICPA vs SEC/PCAOB
sections.content:
**Know the key differences:**

| Area | AICPA | SEC/PCAOB |
|------|-------|------------|
| Partner rotation | Recommended | REQUIRED (5/7 years) |
| Bookkeeping | May be OK with safeguards | PROHIBITED |
| IT systems design | May be OK with safeguards | PROHIBITED |
| Cooling-off (employment) | Not specified | 1 year required |
| Tax services | Generally OK | Restricted for executives |

**Exam tip:** If question says "issuer" → Apply SEC/PCAOB (stricter) rules!

sections.title: Communication Requirements
sections.content:
**Auditor must communicate to audit committee:**

• All critical accounting policies and practices
• Alternative GAAP treatments discussed with management
• Other material written communications with management
• Independence matters (annually)

**Purpose:** Ensure audit committee oversight of auditor independence

sections.title: Integrated Audit Requirement
sections.content:
**For Issuers (accelerated filers):**

**Integrated audit = Audit of BOTH:**
1. Financial statements
2. Internal control over financial reporting (ICFR)

**Same auditor must perform both!**

**Opinion on ICFR:** Material weakness = adverse opinion on ICFR
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: SOX: No Double Dipping!
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. SOX is all about preventing auditors from auditing their own work. Imagine baking a cake, then judging if it's delicious – you're biased! SOX said 'No way!' to auditors doing bookkeeping, IT system design, or valuation services for their public company audit clients. It's a clear conflict of interest. Think of it as a firewall. You're either on the audit side, ensuring compliance, or providing services, not both. This isn't just about rules; it's about maintaining trust in the financial markets. Don't be greedy, stick to the audit!

sections.title: Mixing Up SEC and PCAOB Roles
sections.content: Students often confuse the SEC's and PCAOB's roles. The SEC *sets* the independence rules for auditors of public companies. The PCAOB *enforces* those standards for registered accounting firms. Think of it like this: the SEC is Congress writing the law (SOX) and the PCAOB is the police force making sure everyone follows it. For example, if an auditor violates the independence rules by providing prohibited services, the PCAOB investigates and can impose sanctions. Remembering this distinction can save you on exam day.

sections.title: Sarah's Valuation Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a partner at a mid-sized firm, is thrilled when her audit client, a publicly traded tech company, asks her firm to perform a valuation of their intangible assets for a potential acquisition. Sarah remembers the SOX rules. Even though the valuation work is lucrative, she knows that accepting the engagement would violate auditor independence rules. Therefore, she politely declines the valuation work, preserving her firm's ability to continue auditing the client and upholding ethical standards.

sections.title: Independence Checks: Your First Line of Defense
sections.content: In practice, you'll regularly encounter situations where independence needs careful consideration. When onboarding a new audit client or when the client requests additional services, you must perform a thorough independence check. This involves reviewing the services offered, the relationships between the firm and the client, and ensuring compliance with SEC and PCAOB rules. Document everything! A small oversight can lead to significant penalties and reputational damage for the firm. Proactive independence checks are critical to protecting the integrity of the audit process.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SOX prohibits 9 categories of non-audit services for issuer auditors
Partner rotation: Lead/review = 5 years on, 5 off; Others = 7 on, 2 off
Audit committee must pre-approve ALL audit and non-audit services
1-year cooling-off before engagement team can join client in financial role
Tax services allowed but restricted for executives; aggressive planning prohibited
SEC/PCAOB rules are STRICTER than AICPA rules
Issuers require integrated audit (F/S + ICFR)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following services, if performed for an issuer audit client, would most likely impair an auditor's independence under Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX) and SEC regulations?
options: Preparing the issuer's tax return, with pre-approval from the audit committee.
Providing internal audit outsourcing services to the issuer.
Providing advice on improving the issuer's internal controls over financial reporting.
Assisting the issuer in preparing its quarterly report on Form 10-Q.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Providing internal audit outsourcing services is a prohibited non-audit service under SOX and SEC regulations, as it places the auditor in a position of both designing and auditing the internal controls. This creates a self-review threat to independence. Tax return preparation, with audit committee pre-approval, is generally allowed. Advising on internal controls and assisting with 10-Q preparation (within certain limits and with audit committee pre-approval) are also typically permissible.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which services create a self-review threat or put the auditor in a management role.

reveal.question: List three non-audit services that an auditor of an issuer is prohibited from providing under the Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX).
reveal.answer: Acceptable answers include any three of the following: Bookkeeping and related services; Financial information systems design and implementation; Appraisal or valuation services; Actuarial services; Internal audit outsourcing; Management functions or human resources; Broker-dealer, investment adviser, or investment banking services; Legal services; Expert services unrelated to the audit.

sections.title: SEC vs. PCAOB: Who Does What?

rows.label: Role
values: Sets independence rules for auditors of SEC registrants (issuers).
Enforces auditing standards and related matters for registered accounting firms that audit issuers.

rows.label: Authority
values: Regulatory agency under the U.S. government.
Nonprofit corporation established by SOX.

rows.label: Focus
values: Protecting investors and maintaining fair, orderly, and efficient markets.
Overseeing the audits of public companies to protect investors and further the public interest.

rows.label: Creation
values: Established in 1934
Established by the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002

title: Conflicts of Interest & Threats
description: Identify and resolve conflicts of interest in professional engagements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Conflicts of interest can arise in countless ways—competing clients, personal relationships, financial interests. Knowing how to identify and resolve conflicts protects both you and your clients. The exam tests your ability to spot conflicts and determine appropriate responses!

sections.title: What is a Conflict of Interest?
sections.content:
**Definition:** A circumstance that could impair objectivity or compromise the CPA's ability to fulfill professional responsibilities.

**Types of conflicts:**
• Between CPA and client
• Between two or more clients
• Between CPA's personal interests and professional duties

**Key:** Conflicts must be identified, evaluated, and managed

sections.title: Common Conflict Situations
sections.content:
**Client vs Client conflicts:**
• Providing services to competitors
• Litigation between clients
• M&A transactions with two clients

**Self-interest conflicts:**
• Financial interest in client outcome
• Referral fee arrangements
• Commission-based compensation
• Business relationship with client

sections.title: Conflicts Framework
sections.content:
**Step 1: IDENTIFY the conflict**
• Client relationships
• Personal financial interests
• Business relationships

**Step 2: EVALUATE significance**
• How material is the conflict?
• Are safeguards available?

**Step 3: DETERMINE action**
• Decline engagement
• Implement safeguards
• Obtain informed consent

sections.title: Safeguards for Conflicts
sections.content:
**Organizational safeguards:**
• Separate engagement teams
• Information barriers ("Chinese walls")
• Different chains of command
• Physical separation of files
• Different office locations

**Client-side safeguards:**
• Full disclosure of conflict
• Written informed consent from all affected parties
• Consent to share information (if needed)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Conflict Resolution
sections.content:
**"DICE"** your way through conflicts:

**D**isclose the conflict to affected parties
**I**mplement safeguards
**C**onsent obtained in writing
**E**valuate if safeguards are adequate

**If safeguards aren't enough → DECLINE the engagement!**

sections.title: Informed Consent Requirements
sections.content:
**For valid informed consent:**

1. **Full disclosure:** Explain nature of conflict
2. **Clear communication:** Client understands implications
3. **Written consent:** Document the consent
4. **Voluntary:** No pressure or coercion
5. **Ongoing:** Re-evaluate as circumstances change

**Note:** Some conflicts cannot be resolved even with consent!

sections.title: Unresolvable Conflicts
sections.content:
**Some conflicts require declining the engagement:**

• Independence impairment for attest services
• Significant threat that can't be adequately safeguarded
• One party won't consent
• Legal restrictions prevent dual representation
• CPA's personal integrity would be compromised

**Remember:** Sometimes "no" is the only ethical answer

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Competitor Clients
sections.content:
**Can you serve competing clients?**

**Generally YES, with safeguards:**
• Separate engagement teams
• Confidentiality policies
• Client consent may be needed

**Exception:** Conflict if you have confidential information from one client that could benefit the other

**Exam tip:** Serving competitors ≠ automatic conflict. Evaluate the specific facts!

sections.title: Gift and Hospitality
sections.content:
**Accepting gifts from clients:**

**May be acceptable if:**
• Clearly insignificant and inconsequential
• Customary in business relationships
• No intent to influence judgment

**Generally problematic:**
• Expensive gifts
• Frequent gifts
• Gifts that could appear to impair objectivity

**Best practice:** Decline gifts that could appear improper

sections.title: Documentation
sections.content:
**Document all conflict situations:**

• Nature of conflict identified
• Parties affected
• Evaluation of significance
• Safeguards implemented
• Consents obtained
• Decision made

**Purpose:** Evidence of due care and professional judgment
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Conflict City, Population: You (Maybe!)
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Conflicts of interest? They sound all high and mighty, but they're sneakier than you think. I've seen partners accidentally own stock in a client's competitor – small amount, sure, but still a violation. The key is to be proactive. Firms have conflict checks for a reason. Use them! Don't just assume everything's cool. And if you *think* there might be a conflict, speak up. Better a slightly awkward conversation than a lawsuit. Trust me, I’ve seen both.

sections.title: Client vs. Client: Not Always Obvious!
sections.content: Students often think client vs. client conflicts only exist when clients are DIRECT competitors like Coke vs. Pepsi. That's too narrow! Consider a situation where you're auditing both the buyer and seller in a significant M&A transaction. Your duty to one client (buyer) to find the lowest reasonable price directly conflicts with your duty to the other client (seller) to get the highest possible price. The key difference: it's not always about direct competition, but conflicting objectives arising from your services.

sections.title: Sarah's Stock Options Slip-Up
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, received stock options in a tech startup as part of her compensation package at her firm. Later, her firm was engaged to audit the startup. This creates a self-interest conflict. Even if Sarah isn't directly involved in the audit, her financial interest in the startup's success could impair her objectivity. The lesson: Always disclose personal investments to your firm's ethics department, especially if the company might become a client.

sections.title: Conflicts: More Than Just Audit!
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter conflicts of interest most often during client acceptance and continuance procedures. Before taking on a new client, you MUST run a conflicts check. This involves searching your firm's database for any existing relationships that could create a conflict. For example, if your firm is already representing a company in a lawsuit against a potential new client, you likely can't take on the new client. This is a daily consideration, and mastering it now prevents serious ethical breaches and legal trouble later.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Conflicts can arise between CPA/client, between clients, or with personal interests
Framework: Identify → Evaluate → Determine action
Safeguards include separate teams, information barriers, different offices
Informed consent requires full disclosure, understanding, and written agreement
Some conflicts cannot be resolved even with consent → must decline
Serving competitors is possible with proper safeguards
Document all conflict evaluations and resolutions

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Knowledge Check: Identifying Conflicts of Interest

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following situations most clearly represents a conflict of interest for a CPA performing audit services for a client?
options: The CPA previously prepared the client's tax returns.
The CPA owns a small number of shares in a mutual fund that holds stock in the client.
The CPA's spouse is the Chief Financial Officer of a major competitor of the client.
The client is a privately held company and the CPA provides only audit services.
E. The CPA provides tax services to the client.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is C. A spouse's role as CFO of a major competitor creates a significant conflict of interest, as the CPA's objectivity could be impaired due to divided loyalties and potential access to confidential information from both entities. A, B, D, and E are less likely to significantly impair objectivity. Preparing taxes or owning shares in a mutual fund is not necessarily a conflict.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which situation creates the most direct threat to the CPA's objectivity and independence.

reveal.question: List the three steps in the conflicts framework, in order.
reveal.answer: 1. IDENTIFY the conflict. 2. EVALUATE the significance. 3. DETERMINE the action.

rows.label: Definition
values: Conflict arising from serving multiple clients with potentially adverse interests.
Conflict arising from the CPA's personal financial or business interests potentially impairing objectivity.

rows.label: Example
values: Representing both the buyer and seller in a merger transaction.
Holding a direct financial interest in the client's company.

rows.label: Mitigation
values: Disclosure and consent from both clients, implementation of safeguards.
Divesting the financial interest, declining the engagement.

title: Engagement Letters & Acceptance
description: Understand the requirements for accepting engagements and documenting terms

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Before accepting ANY engagement, auditors must evaluate whether they should take the job. The engagement letter then formalizes the agreement and sets expectations. Understanding this process is crucial for audit planning questions on AUD!

sections.title: Pre-Engagement Activities
sections.content:
**Before accepting an engagement, evaluate:**

**1. Client acceptance/continuance:**
• Client integrity
• Independence issues
• Competence to perform engagement
• Predecessor auditor communications

**2. Engagement acceptance:**
• Appropriate reporting framework
• Purpose of engagement
• Nature of opinion expected

sections.title: Client Acceptance Procedures
sections.content:
**Evaluate client integrity:**
• Background investigation
• Inquiries with bankers, attorneys, credit agencies
• Financial stability of client
• Reputation in business community

**Why this matters:** Dishonest client = higher fraud risk

sections.title: Predecessor Auditor Communication
sections.content:
**REQUIRED before accepting engagement:**

**Successor auditor should inquire about:**
• Reasons for change in auditors
• Disagreements with management
• Management integrity issues
• Significant accounting issues
• Communications with audit committee

**Note:** Client must authorize predecessor to respond. If client refuses → consider red flag!

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Client Acceptance
sections.content:
**"RICH" clients are worth accepting:**

**R**eputation - What's client's standing?
**I**ntegrity - Is management honest?
**C**ompetence - Can we do the work?
**H**istory - What did predecessor say?

**If any are questionable → Think twice before accepting!**

sections.title: Purpose of Engagement Letter
sections.content:
**The engagement letter:**
• Establishes terms of engagement
• Documents understanding with client
• Reduces risk of misunderstandings
• Creates contract between parties

**Required for ALL engagements!**
• Audit, review, compilation
• Initial and recurring engagements
• Terms may be updated annually

sections.title: Required Engagement Letter Contents
sections.content:
**Essential elements:**

1. **Objective and scope** of the audit
2. **Responsibilities of the auditor**
3. **Responsibilities of management**
4. **Identification of framework** (e.g., GAAP)
5. **Expected form of reports**
6. **Limitations** (sampling, inherent risks)
7. **Fees and billing** arrangements

sections.title: Management Responsibilities
sections.content:
**Management must acknowledge responsibility for:**

• Preparation of F/S in accordance with framework
• Design and maintenance of internal controls
• Prevention and detection of fraud
• Identifying and disclosing related party transactions
• Compliance with laws and regulations
• Providing access to all information
• **Written representations to auditor**

sections.title: Auditor Responsibilities
sections.content:
**The engagement letter confirms auditor will:**

• Conduct audit in accordance with GAAS
• Obtain reasonable assurance about material misstatement
• Issue opinion on financial statements
• Communicate significant findings
• Maintain independence and objectivity

**Note:** Auditor does NOT guarantee detection of all fraud!

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Limiting Auditor Responsibility
sections.content:
**Cannot limit liability through engagement letter for:**
• Gross negligence
• Fraud
• Willful misconduct

**May limit liability:**
• Some jurisdictions allow caps on damages
• Alternative dispute resolution clauses

**Key:** Cannot contract away responsibility to follow GAAS!

sections.title: Changes in Engagement Terms
sections.content:
**Client requests to change engagement (e.g., audit to review):**

**Consider:**
• Reason for request (legitimate vs. inappropriate)
• Additional effort already completed
• Cost of work already performed

**Inappropriate reasons:**
• Scope limitation auditor can't overcome
• Client trying to hide information
• Disagreement over accounting treatment

**If inappropriate → Withdraw, don't downgrade!**

sections.title: Recurring Audits
sections.content:
**For continuing clients:**

**Assess whether engagement letter needs updating:**
• Changes in client structure
• Changes in accounting framework
• New regulatory requirements
• Changes in engagement scope

**At minimum:** Send letter confirming terms remain in effect

**Best practice:** New engagement letter each year
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Client Integrity: Gut Check Time!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. You're about to sign up for weeks (or months!) of audit hell. Before you dive in, that 'client integrity' thing? HUGE. I've seen firms get burned because they ignored red flags. A whispered rumor about shady deals? A CEO who won't make eye contact? Trust your gut. Walk away if something feels off. It's way easier to find a new client than to defend yourself in court because you ignored your spidey-sense. Your license is worth more than their fees!

sections.title: Forgetting the Predecessor Auditor Call
sections.content: A common mistake is skipping or rushing the communication with the predecessor auditor. Students think it's just a formality, but it's crucial! Failing to adequately inquire about management's integrity, disagreements over accounting principles, or internal control weaknesses can lead you to accept a risky engagement. Remember, the predecessor auditor holds valuable insights. Properly communicating with them avoids inheriting a ticking time bomb and helps in evaluating your own acceptance decision.

sections.title: Sarah's Independence Scare
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, discovered her firm had a small investment in a potential audit client, 'Tech Solutions Inc.' She knew independence was crucial, but the investment was tiny – only $500. However, she remembered the rules and immediately flagged it to her manager. The firm divested the stock before accepting the engagement, avoiding a major independence violation and a potential SEC investigation. Always remember even small investments can impair independence!

sections.title: Engagement Letters: Your Audit Shield
sections.content: In practice, you'll use engagement letters constantly. Every new client, every year. Think of it as your audit shield. It clearly defines the scope of the audit, management's responsibilities, and your firm's responsibilities. A well-drafted engagement letter can prevent disputes down the line, like scope creep (e.g., management expecting a full fraud investigation when you only agreed to a financial statement audit). Template engagement letters are a good starting point, but ALWAYS tailor them to the specific engagement!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Pre-engagement: Evaluate client integrity, independence, competence
Communication with predecessor auditor REQUIRED before accepting
If client won't authorize predecessor communication → red flag
Engagement letter documents terms, responsibilities, fees
Management responsible for F/S, controls, fraud prevention
Cannot contract away GAAS responsibilities
Client request to downgrade engagement may signal problem

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Before accepting a new audit engagement, a successor auditor is required to make specific inquiries of the predecessor auditor. Which of the following inquiries is *least* likely to be made by the successor auditor?
options: Facts that might bear on the integrity of management.
Disagreements the predecessor auditor had with management concerning auditing procedures and accounting principles.
The predecessor auditor's evaluation of inherent risk and control risk.
Communications the predecessor auditor had with the audit committee regarding fraud, illegal acts, and internal control matters.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While the successor auditor should understand the risks identified by the predecessor, they are not *required* to obtain the predecessor's specific risk assessments. The other options are required inquiries related to management integrity, disagreements, and communications with the audit committee.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which inquiries are *required* to assess the appropriateness of accepting the engagement versus understanding the risk environment.

reveal.question: List three topics the successor auditor should inquire about with the predecessor auditor before accepting an engagement.
reveal.answer:
1. Reasons for the change in auditors.
2. Disagreements with management.
3. Management integrity issues.
4. Significant accounting issues.
5. Communications with the audit committee (any 3 of these).

rows.label: Focus
values: Overall suitability of the client; assessing client integrity and risk profile.
Assessing the specific engagement's appropriateness, scope, and feasibility.

rows.label: Primary Concerns
values: Client's honesty, ethical behavior, and financial stability.
Appropriateness of the reporting framework, the purpose of the engagement, and the nature/scope of the services.

rows.label: Example Activities
values: Background checks, inquiries with third parties (bankers, attorneys), predecessor auditor communication.
Determining if the auditor has the necessary expertise, if the scope is adequately defined, and if the client understands their responsibilities.

title: Communications with Governance
description: Learn the required communications between auditors and those charged with governance

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Effective two-way communication between auditors and the audit committee/board is essential for a successful audit. The exam frequently tests WHAT must be communicated, WHEN, and in what FORM!

sections.title: Those Charged with Governance
sections.content:
**Definition:** Person(s) with responsibility for overseeing strategic direction and accountability of the entity.

**Examples:**
• Board of directors
• Audit committee
• Supervisory board
• Trustees (NFP)
• General partners

**Key:** May differ from management (who runs day-to-day operations)

sections.title: Purpose of Communications
sections.content:
**Two-way communication helps:**

**Auditor:**
• Obtain information relevant to the audit
• Understand entity governance
• Assess control environment

**Governance:**
• Understand audit scope and timing
• Oversee financial reporting process
• Respond to findings and issues

sections.title: Required Communications: Planning
sections.content:
**Early in the audit, communicate:**

• Planned scope and timing of audit
• Auditor's responsibilities
• Nature of communications expected
• Significant risks identified
• Significant areas requiring judgment
• Materiality concepts

**Purpose:** Set expectations and coordinate

sections.title: Required Communications: Findings
sections.content:
**After audit procedures, communicate:**

• Significant accounting policies (including changes)
• Significant accounting estimates and judgments
• Audit adjustments (proposed and passed)
• Uncorrected misstatements
• Disagreements with management
• Significant difficulties encountered
• Material weaknesses and significant deficiencies

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: What to Communicate
sections.content:
**"SUDDIM"** - things that make governance go "hmm":

**S**ignificant findings
**U**ncorrected misstatements
**D**isagreements with management
**D**ifficulties (scope limitations)
**I**nternal control deficiencies
**M**anagement representations (required letters)

**Think:** Anything governance SHOULD know to fulfill their oversight role!

sections.title: Significant Audit Findings
sections.content:
**Must communicate:**

**1. Qualitative aspects of accounting:**
• Acceptability of significant policies
• Subjectivity in sensitive estimates
• Consistency of disclosures

**2. Significant difficulties:**
• Delays in providing information
• Unreasonable time pressures
• Scope limitations

**3. Written representations obtained**

sections.title: Internal Control Deficiencies
sections.content:
**Must communicate IN WRITING:**

**Material weakness:** Reasonable possibility that material misstatement won't be prevented/detected timely

**Significant deficiency:** Less severe than material weakness, but warrants attention

**Timing:** Communicate promptly, no later than 60 days after report release (for nonissuers)

**For issuers:** Material weaknesses reported publicly

sections.title: Fraud Communications
sections.content:
**Must communicate:**

**To management:** All fraud involving employees (unless trivial)

**To governance:** 
• Fraud involving management
• Fraud causing material misstatement
• Other fraud, unless clearly inconsequential

**To regulatory authorities:** When required by law

**Note:** If fraud involves senior management → communicate directly to audit committee

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Timing of Communications
sections.content:
**Timing varies by matter:**

**Before audit:** Planned scope, timing, responsibilities

**During audit:** Fraud, significant difficulties, independence issues

**End of audit:** Uncorrected misstatements, control deficiencies

**After report:** Written IC deficiencies (within 60 days for nonissuers)

**Key:** Significant matters should be communicated TIMELY!

sections.title: Independence Communication
sections.content:
**For Issuers (SEC/PCAOB):**

**Must communicate IN WRITING, at least annually:**
• All relationships with the client
• Matters affecting independence
• Fees for audit and non-audit services
• Independence confirmed

**For Nonissuers:** Communication expected but less formal
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Governance is NOT Management, Folks!
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Governance vs. Management gets tricky. Think of it this way: management is down in the trenches, making the daily calls. Governance is the high-level strategy, the board room view. They're supposed to be asking the tough questions, like, 'Are we REALLY sure about this new market?' or 'Is our internal controls system actually working?' As auditors, we need to know who's who because communicating material weaknesses to the wrong group is a major no-no. Imagine telling the CFO about a control issue when it should be the audit committee. Awkward!

sections.title: Forgetting the 'Two-Way Street' in Communication
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking communication with governance is just a one-way street – the auditor informing them of findings. It's actually a two-way dialogue! Students often overlook the auditor's need to *obtain* information from governance, like their views on management integrity or understanding the overall risk appetite. Failing to solicit this information can lead to a superficial audit and missed red flags. Remember, governance is a key source of information, and we need to actively engage them to understand the full picture.

sections.title: Sarah's Surprise: Governance's Concerns
sections.content: Sarah, a newly promoted audit manager, was reviewing the communication log for a recent audit. She noticed minimal documentation of discussions with the audit committee. When she asked the senior auditor, he said, 'They just rubber-stamp everything!' Sarah knew this was a problem. She explained that even if the audit committee seems passive, documenting their questions, concerns (especially about fraud risks or going concern issues), and management's responses is crucial for audit quality and defense.

sections.title: Real World: Planning Your Governance Chat
sections.content: In practice, before even starting fieldwork, you'll schedule a preliminary meeting with the audit committee or equivalent. Use this time to understand their key concerns, recent events affecting the company, and their expectations for the audit. This proactive approach not only satisfies auditing standards but also builds trust and rapport. Think of it as setting the stage for a more effective and efficient audit. Skipping this step can lead to missed risks and a less valuable audit for the client.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Those charged with governance = oversight responsibility (board, audit committee)
Two-way communication benefits both auditor and governance
Communicate planned scope, timing, significant risks BEFORE audit
Communicate findings, deficiencies, disagreements AFTER procedures
Material weaknesses and significant deficiencies must be IN WRITING
Fraud involving management → communicate directly to audit committee
Independence communications required in writing for issuers

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the MOST accurate description of 'those charged with governance'?
options: Management personnel responsible for the day-to-day operations of the entity.
Individuals or groups responsible for overseeing the strategic direction and accountability of the entity.
The internal audit department responsible for monitoring internal controls.
External stakeholders such as creditors and investors.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Those charged with governance are responsible for overseeing the strategic direction and accountability of the entity. Management handles the day-to-day operations.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about who sets the overall direction and holds management accountable, not who executes the daily tasks.

reveal.question: List two ways two-way communication with governance benefits the auditor.
reveal.answer: 1. Obtain information relevant to the audit. 2. Understand entity governance. 3. Assess the control environment. (Any two of these)

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following topics is an auditor required to communicate to those charged with governance early in the audit?
options: A detailed breakdown of all audit procedures to be performed.
The auditor's opinion on the effectiveness of internal control.
The planned scope and timing of the audit.
A list of all immaterial misstatements detected during the audit.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Early communication with governance includes the planned scope and timing of the audit to set expectations and facilitate coordination.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on what needs to be discussed upfront to ensure a smooth and effective audit process. Think about setting the stage.

rows.label: Responsibility
values: Day-to-day operations
Strategic direction and accountability

rows.label: Example
values: CEO, CFO
Board of Directors, Audit Committee

rows.label: Focus
values: Execution
Oversight

title: Quality Management Standards (SQMS)
description: Understand the firm-wide quality management system requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Quality management at the FIRM level ensures that every engagement meets professional standards. The new SQMS standards (replacing QC sections) require a risk-based approach to quality. Understanding these standards shows how audit quality is maintained across an entire practice!

sections.title: What is Quality Management?
sections.content:
**System of Quality Management (SQM):**

**Purpose:** Design, implement, and operate a system providing reasonable assurance that:
• Firm and personnel fulfill professional responsibilities
• Reports issued are appropriate
• Professional standards are followed

**Key change:** Risk-based approach (identify risks → design responses)

sections.title: SQMS Structure
sections.content:
**Two SQMS Standards:**

**SQMS No. 1:** Overall quality management for the firm
• Establishes components of the system
• Risk assessment and response
• Monitoring activities

**SQMS No. 2:** Engagement quality reviews
• When required
• Qualifications of reviewer
• Nature of review procedures

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Quality Components
sections.content:
**"GRACEFUL RIM"**

**G**overnance and leadership
**R**isk assessment
**A**cceptance and continuance
**C**ommunication and information
**E**thical requirements
**F**irm resources
**U**sing engagement quality reviews
**L**... (Engagement performance/Learning)
**R**esources
**I**nformation and communication
**M**onitoring and remediation

sections.title: Governance and Leadership
sections.content:
**Firm leadership must:**

• Demonstrate commitment to quality
• Assign responsibility for quality management
• Establish culture that emphasizes quality
• Integrate quality into strategic decisions
• Ensure adequate resources for quality

**Key:** Tone at the top drives audit quality!

sections.title: Engagement Performance
sections.content:
**Policies and procedures for:**

**Direction:** Clear engagement objectives, team understanding

**Supervision:** Ongoing oversight during engagement

**Review:** Timely evaluation of work performed

**Consultation:** Obtaining advice on difficult issues

**Differences of opinion:** Process for resolving disagreements

sections.title: Engagement Quality Review
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Objective evaluation of significant judgments and conclusions

**Required for:**
• Audits of issuers (public companies)
• Audits where required by law/regulation
• Other engagements meeting firm's criteria

**Reviewer qualifications:**
• Technical competence
• Experience and authority
• Objectivity (not involved in engagement)

sections.title: EQ Review Procedures
sections.content:
**Engagement quality reviewer evaluates:**

• Significant risks and responses
• Significant judgments (estimates, accounting policies)
• Significant matters for governance
• Uncorrected misstatements
• Matters communicated to management/governance
• F/S and proposed report
• Independence conclusion

**Report not released until EQR completed!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: EQR vs Partner Review
sections.content:
**Engagement QUALITY review (EQR):**
• Separate from engagement team
• Objective evaluation
• Required for certain engagements
• Reviews significant judgments

**Engagement PARTNER review:**
• Part of engagement team
• Responsible for overall audit
• Reviews work of team
• Signs the report

**Key:** EQR provides independent "second look"!

sections.title: Monitoring and Remediation
sections.content:
**Monitoring activities:**
• Ongoing evaluation of quality system
• Periodic inspection of completed engagements
• External inspections (peer review, PCAOB)

**When deficiencies found:**
• Evaluate root cause
• Design remedial actions
• Implement changes
• Evaluate effectiveness

**Annual evaluation:** Overall system operating effectively?

sections.title: Documentation
sections.content:
**Firm must document:**

• Quality objectives and risks
• Policies and procedures (responses)
• Monitoring activities performed
• Deficiencies identified and remediation
• Annual evaluation and conclusions
• Assignment of responsibility

**Retention:** Long enough to enable monitoring and evaluation
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: SQMS: Don't Just Check Boxes, Think!
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. SQMS can feel like another compliance hurdle. But think of it this way: it's about protecting yourself and the firm. Don't just blindly follow the checklist. Really consider the risks to quality for *your* specific engagements. I've seen firms that just go through the motions, and when something goes wrong, they're completely exposed because their system wasn't actually tailored to their practice. Spend the time upfront to understand your risks and build a system that addresses them. It pays off in the long run, trust me.

sections.title: Confusing SQMS No. 1 & SQMS No. 2
sections.content: Students often confuse SQMS No. 1 (firm-wide quality management) with SQMS No. 2 (engagement level). The key difference is scope. SQMS No. 1 is about the *entire* firm's system for ensuring quality across all engagements. It sets the stage. SQMS No. 2 focuses on implementing quality management procedures *on a specific audit or attestation engagement*. Think of SQMS No. 1 as the blueprint and SQMS No. 2 as the construction crew following those plans on a particular project.

sections.title: Sarah's SQMS Surprise
sections.content: Sarah, a new partner, discovered her firm's SQMS didn't address cybersecurity risks adequately. Their risk assessment hadn't considered the increasing threat of ransomware attacks on client data. Sarah spearheaded a project to update the SQMS, implementing new controls like mandatory cybersecurity training and enhanced data encryption protocols. This proactive step not only strengthened their quality management system but also protected their clients and the firm's reputation.

sections.title: SQMS in Daily Life: Engagement Acceptance
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter SQMS principles every time you evaluate whether to accept a new client or engagement. The firm's risk assessment procedures, part of SQMS No. 1, should inform your decision-making. Do you have the expertise? Are there independence issues? What's the client's reputation? These are all crucial SQMS considerations that help ensure you're taking on engagements that align with the firm's quality standards and minimize potential risks. Neglecting these steps can lead to costly and damaging consequences down the line.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: SQMS requires risk-based approach to quality management
Eight components from governance to monitoring
Leadership sets tone; culture emphasizes quality over profit
Engagement quality review required for issuers and high-risk engagements
EQR is objective, independent evaluation of significant judgments
Report cannot be released until EQR is complete
Monitoring includes ongoing evaluation and periodic inspections

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following is the PRIMARY focus of SQMS No. 1?
options: Performing engagement quality reviews on specific engagements.
Establishing and maintaining a comprehensive system of quality management at the firm level.
Determining the independence of the engagement partner.
Documenting the auditor's understanding of the client's internal control.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: SQMS No. 1 focuses on the overall quality management system for the firm, covering risk assessment, responses, and monitoring activities to ensure the firm meets professional responsibilities and issues appropriate reports.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the scope of the standard – is it engagement-specific or firm-wide?

sections.title: Purpose of System of Quality Management (SQM)

reveal.question: What are the two main objectives a System of Quality Management (SQM) aims to achieve?
reveal.answer:
1. Provide reasonable assurance that the firm and its personnel fulfill their professional responsibilities in accordance with relevant professional requirements and legal and regulatory requirements, and conduct engagements in accordance with professional standards.
2. Ensure that reports issued by the firm or engagement partners are appropriate in the circumstances.

rows.label: Scope
values: Overall quality management for the firm
Engagement quality reviews

rows.label: Focus
values: Establishes components of the system, risk assessment and response, monitoring activities
When reviews are required, qualifications of the reviewer, nature of review procedures

rows.label: Applicability
values: Applies to all firms performing audits, reviews, and compilations.
Applies to specific engagements based on firm policy or regulatory requirements.

title: Professional Skepticism & Judgment
description: Develop the critical mindset essential for effective auditing

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Professional skepticism is the auditor's most important mindset. It means questioning everything—not assuming information is true just because management says so. Many audit failures trace back to insufficient skepticism. This concept permeates EVERY aspect of auditing!

sections.title: What is Professional Skepticism?
sections.content:
**Definition:** An attitude that includes:
• A questioning mind
• Being alert to conditions indicating possible misstatement
• Critical assessment of audit evidence

**NOT about:** Assuming dishonesty
**IT IS about:** Neither assuming honesty NOR dishonesty

**Maintain throughout the audit!**

sections.title: Elements of Skepticism
sections.content:
**A questioning mind:**
• Why did this transaction occur?
• Does this make business sense?
• What could go wrong?

**Alert to red flags:**
• Unusual transactions or amounts
• Missing documentation
• Inconsistent explanations

**Critical assessment:**
• Is evidence sufficient and appropriate?
• Does evidence from different sources agree?
• Are management's assertions reasonable?

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Skepticism Framework
sections.content:
**"QUEST" for truth:**

**Q**uestion everything
**U**nderstand the business rationale
**E**valuate evidence critically
**S**eek corroboration
**T**hink independently (don't assume)

**Remember:** Trust but VERIFY!

sections.title: What is Professional Judgment?
sections.content:
**Definition:** Application of relevant training, knowledge, and experience to make informed decisions about courses of action.

**Used throughout the audit for:**
• Planning the audit
• Performing procedures
• Evaluating evidence
• Drawing conclusions

**Not arbitrary:** Based on facts, knowledge of client, professional standards

sections.title: Applying Professional Judgment
sections.content:
**Good judgment requires:**

**1. Sufficient knowledge:**
• Technical accounting and auditing standards
• Client's industry and business
• Applicable regulations

**2. Relevant experience:**
• Similar situations encountered
• Lessons learned from past engagements

**3. Consultation:**
• Seek input on complex issues
• Use firm resources and specialists

sections.title: Skepticism in Specific Areas
sections.content:
**Areas requiring heightened skepticism:**

**Estimates:**
• Management bias potential
• Significant assumptions
• Wide range of possible values

**Related parties:**
• Unusual transactions
• Terms not arm's length

**Revenue recognition:**
• Presumed fraud risk
• Complex arrangements

**Journal entries:**
• Unusual or non-routine
• Period-end adjustments

sections.title: Impediments to Skepticism
sections.content:
**Barriers to maintaining skepticism:**

**Time pressure:** Deadlines may discourage thorough questioning

**Familiarity:** Long relationship with client can reduce alertness

**Confirmation bias:** Seeking evidence that supports initial belief

**Overreliance:** Trusting client explanations without verification

**Workload:** Fatigue can reduce critical thinking

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Documentation of Skepticism
sections.content:
**Documentation should reflect skeptical approach:**

• How evidence was evaluated
• Alternative explanations considered
• Why auditor's conclusion is appropriate
• How contradictory evidence was resolved

**If documentation doesn't show skepticism:**
→ Appears auditor just "went through the motions"
→ Vulnerable in litigation and inspection

sections.title: Skepticism and Fraud
sections.content:
**Fraud consideration requires heightened skepticism:**

• Assume risk of fraud exists
• Consider how fraud COULD occur
• Don't dismiss red flags as errors
• Question unusual explanations
• Verify management explanations

**Remember:** Most fraud involves management override of controls
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Skepticism: My First Audit Disaster (Almost!)
sections.content: Okay, picture this: fresh-faced me, first audit, client's a small construction company. Everything *looked* legit. Profit margins were good, documentation was there. I almost signed off. Then, a nagging feeling. I dug deeper into a 'miscellaneous expense' account with $50k in undocumented payments. Turns out, it was the owner's personal gambling debts! Professional skepticism isn't about distrusting everyone, it's about having that 'wait, what' moment and actually investigating. Saved the firm a lawsuit, and me a serious talking-to.

sections.title: Honesty vs. Skepticism: The Fatal Flaw
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking professional skepticism means automatically assuming the client is lying or committing fraud. That's not it! You don't walk in guns blazing. The problem arises when you blindly accept everything at face value *because* you assume everyone is inherently honest. It's a balance. You need to verify and corroborate, even if things seem alright. Think of it like this: verify first, *then* assess the integrity of the explanation. Don't let your initial assumptions cloud your judgment.

sections.title: Sarah's Inventory Snafu: The Missing Boxes
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, was reviewing inventory at a tech company. The inventory count matched the system records, but Sarah noticed a discrepancy: several boxes of high-value components were listed as being in a specific location, but weren't physically there. Instead of just accepting the warehouse manager's explanation of 'misplacement', she pushed for a thorough investigation. Turns out, it was a small-scale theft operation. Skepticism, not accusation, uncovered the problem.

sections.title: Spotting Red Flags in Bank Reconciliations
sections.content: In practice, you'll use professional skepticism constantly during bank reconciliations. Look for large or unusual reconciling items, especially outstanding checks that are several months old. A $10,000 outstanding check from 6 months ago? Red flag! Investigate. Also be alert for items labeled 'miscellaneous' or 'adjustment' without detailed explanations. These are prime spots for hiding errors or irregularities. Mastering this now will save you hours of rework and potential embarrassment later.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
