Mastering this process saves time and resources by ensuring you're focusing on the areas with the highest risk of material misstatement. Properly documented, your evaluation supports the basis for your opinion.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Investigate cause of every deviation/error found
Project sample results to the population
Consider sampling risk in your conclusion
Anomalies can be excluded only with strong documentation
Compare projected results to tolerable threshold
If over threshold: expand testing, adjust, or qualify
Document everything—errors, projections, conclusions

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During the evaluation of an attribute sampling application, an auditor discovers a deviation from a prescribed control procedure. The auditor determines that this deviation is an anomaly. Which of the following actions is most appropriate?
options: Project the deviation rate to the population and consider the effect on the planned audit procedures.
Exclude the deviation when projecting the deviation rate to the population if the auditor has established that the deviation does not represent a deviation in the population.
Increase the sample size to reduce the risk of assessing control risk too low.
Communicate the deviation to management and recommend immediate corrective action.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: If the auditor has determined that the deviation is an anomaly and does not represent deviations in the population, it is appropriate to exclude the deviation when projecting the deviation rate. This is because the anomaly is not representative of the population as a whole. Projecting it would skew the results and lead to an inaccurate assessment of control risk.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the definition of an anomaly in sampling.

reveal.question: List the four steps an auditor should take when evaluating sample results.
reveal.answer: 1. Investigate each deviation/misstatement found. 2. Project results to the population. 3. Consider sampling risk. 4. Conclude on the test objective.

sections.title: Attribute Sampling vs. Variables Sampling: Projection

rows.label: Projection Method
values: Sample deviation rate = Deviations found / Sample size
Projection based on: Known misstatement (specific items tested), Projected misstatement (sample × projection factor), Total estimated misstatement

rows.label: Focus
values: Deviation rate from a control
Monetary misstatement

title: Auditing Accounting Estimates
description: Learn the special considerations for testing management estimates

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Estimates are EVERYWHERE in financial statements—allowances, fair values, depreciation, pensions, contingencies. They involve significant judgment and are prone to bias. AS 2501/AU-C 540 requires specific procedures. Estimates are a favorite exam topic because they test professional judgment!

sections.title: What Are Accounting Estimates?
sections.content:
**Definition:**
Approximations of monetary amounts in the absence of a precise means of measurement.

**Common examples:**
• Allowance for doubtful accounts
• Inventory obsolescence reserve
• Warranty liabilities
• Fair value measurements
• Depreciation/amortization
• Pension obligations
• Contingent liabilities

sections.title: Why Estimates Are Risky
sections.content:
**Inherently uncertain:**
• Subjective by nature
• Depend on assumptions
• Outcome unknown when estimated

**Susceptible to bias:**
• Management incentives
• Optimistic or pessimistic
• May be intentional or unintentional

**Often significant risk area!**

sections.title: Required Procedures
sections.content:
**Auditor must:**

**1. Identify estimates** that may be misstated
**2. Understand management's process:**
   • Method used
   • Key assumptions
   • Data inputs
   • Controls over estimation

**3. Evaluate reasonableness** of estimate

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Testing Estimates
sections.content:
**"MADE"** tests estimates:

**M**ethod—Is approach appropriate?
**A**ssumptions—Are they reasonable?
**D**ata—Is underlying data accurate?
**E**valuate outcome—Does estimate make sense?

**Every estimate was MADE by management—question how!**

sections.title: Three Approaches to Testing
sections.content:
**Choose one or combination:**

**1. Test management's process:**
• Evaluate method used
• Test data inputs
• Assess assumptions

**2. Develop independent estimate:**
• Use different method or assumptions
• Compare to management's estimate

**3. Review subsequent events:**
• What actually happened?
• Does outcome support estimate?

sections.title: Evaluating Assumptions
sections.content:
**Key questions:**

**Are assumptions:**
• Relevant and reliable?
• Consistent with prior periods?
• Consistent with industry?
• Internally consistent?
• Supported by evidence?

**Consider:**
• Sensitivity to changes
• Alternative assumptions
• Bias indicators

sections.title: Management Bias
sections.content:
**Indicators of potential bias:**

• Change in method without explanation
• Consistently optimistic assumptions
• Estimates at edge of acceptable range
• Contradicts available evidence
• Selective use of information

**Response:**
• Heightened skepticism
• Consider management intent
• Evaluate in context of other estimates

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Point vs. Range
sections.content:
**Auditor may develop a range:**

• If management's estimate falls WITHIN auditor's range → acceptable
• If management's estimate is OUTSIDE auditor's range → likely misstatement

**Important:** Auditor's range must be narrow enough to be useful!

**All amounts in range must be individually reasonable**

sections.title: Estimation Uncertainty
sections.content:
**Higher estimation uncertainty:**
• Complex calculations
• Long forecast period
• Lack of reliable data
• Significant assumptions required

**Response to high uncertainty:**
• May be significant risk
• More extensive procedures
• Evaluate disclosure adequacy
• Consider emphasis of matter

sections.title: Documentation
sections.content:
**Document:**

• Basis for identifying significant estimates
• Understanding of management's process
• Procedures performed and approach used
• Evaluation of assumptions
• Assessment of estimation uncertainty
• Consideration of bias indicators
• Conclusions on reasonableness
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Estimates: Where the Audit Rubber Meets the Road
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Auditing estimates? It's where the audit gets *interesting*. Management's basically saying, "Yeah, we kinda guessed at this number." Your job is to figure out if that guess is reasonable. It's not about finding the *exact* right number (because, duh, it's an estimate!), but making sure it's within a reasonable range and based on solid assumptions. Think of it like Goldilocks and the porridge – not too hot, not too cold, just right. And watch out for management's incentives; everyone wants to look good!

sections.title: Assuming Estimates are Always 'Wrong'
sections.content: A frequent error is assuming estimates are inherently incorrect because they're not precise. Remember, estimates are *approximations* in the absence of precise measurement. The goal isn't to find the 'correct' number, but to evaluate the reasonableness and support for management's estimate. Students often waste time trying to pinpoint an exact figure, missing the bigger picture of assessing the process, assumptions, and potential biases. Focus on evaluating the data and methodology used to arrive at the estimate, not necessarily disproving the estimate itself. It's about reasonable assurance, not absolute certainty.

sections.title: Sarah's Sticky Warranty Situation
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, is reviewing a software company's warranty liability. Management has significantly decreased the warranty reserve this year, citing improved software quality. However, Sarah notices a recent surge in customer complaints about a new feature. While the company claims the issues are minor, Sarah needs to independently assess if the reduced warranty reserve is still reasonable given this new information and potential future warranty claims, or if management is downplaying a material risk to boost profits.

sections.title: Estimates: A Daily Dose of Audit Judgment
sections.content: You'll encounter accounting estimates constantly. Analyzing the allowance for doubtful accounts at a small retailer, or valuing complex financial instruments at a hedge fund - they're everywhere. Mastering this now means you can quickly identify potential areas of risk, evaluate management's assumptions, and design effective audit procedures. Think about it: a seemingly small bias in an estimate can have a huge impact on the financial statements. Being able to sniff out those potential issues is what separates a good auditor from a great one. This is where your professional skepticism shines!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Estimates involve judgment and inherent uncertainty
Understand management's process, method, and assumptions
Three approaches: Test process, develop independent estimate, review subsequent events
Evaluate assumptions for reasonableness and bias
Management bias indicators: consistency, contradicting evidence, edge of range
Auditor's range must be reasonably narrow
High estimation uncertainty may indicate significant risk

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following would LEAST likely be considered an accounting estimate requiring audit focus?
options: The annual depreciation expense for a building using the straight-line method.
The warranty liability for products sold during the year.
The allowance for doubtful accounts based on an aging of accounts receivable.
The fair value of a complex derivative financial instrument traded on an active exchange.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While fair value measurements can be accounting estimates, a derivative traded on an active exchange has a readily available market price, making it less subjective and inherently uncertain than the other options. The other options require significant judgment and assumptions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which item has the MOST readily available objective data.

reveal.question: Name the three primary procedures an auditor MUST perform when auditing accounting estimates.
reveal.answer:
1. Identify estimates that may be misstated.
2. Understand management's process (method, assumptions, data inputs, controls).
3. Evaluate the reasonableness of the estimate.

knowledgeCheck.question: When auditing an accounting estimate, an auditor's understanding of management's process should include all of the following EXCEPT:
options: The method used to develop the estimate.
The key assumptions underlying the estimate.
The data inputs used in developing the estimate.
Management's compensation structure related to the estimate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While understanding management's incentives is important for assessing bias, the auditor's understanding of management's process focuses on the methodology, assumptions, and data used to create the estimate itself, not management's compensation.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Focus on what directly impacts the CREATION of the estimate.

title: Related Party Transactions
description: Identify and audit transactions with related parties

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Related parties can hide fraud, manipulate earnings, and deceive users! These transactions may not be arm's-length and often require special disclosure. Understanding how to identify and audit related party transactions is critical—they're a favorite tool for financial statement fraudsters!

sections.title: What Are Related Parties?
sections.content:
**Definition (per GAAP):**

**Related parties include:**
• Affiliates (parent, subsidiary)
• Principal owners and families
• Management and families
• Entities under common control
• Equity method investees
• Employee benefit plans
• Any party that can significantly influence

sections.title: Why Related Parties Matter
sections.content:
**Risks:**

• Transactions may not be arm's-length
• Terms may not reflect fair value
• Undisclosed relationships
• Fraud facilitated through related parties
• Off-balance sheet arrangements

**Disclosure required** for material transactions!
• Nature of relationship
• Description of transactions
• Dollar amounts

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Related Party Risk
sections.content:
**"SHAM"** transactions warning:

**S**ubstance over form—real transaction?
**H**idden relationships—who's involved?
**A**rm's-length?—fair terms?
**M**aterial disclosure—is it complete?

**Related party deals can be a SHAM!**

sections.title: Auditor Responsibilities
sections.content:
**Required procedures:**

**1. Obtain understanding:**
• Names of known related parties
• Nature of relationships
• Types of transactions

**2. Remain alert** throughout audit

**3. Evaluate:**
• Business purpose
• Appropriate accounting
• Adequate disclosure

sections.title: Identifying Related Parties
sections.content:
**Procedures to identify:**

• Inquire of management
• Review prior year workpapers
• Examine SEC filings
• Review shareholder records
• Inspect material contracts
• Review Board minutes
• Inquire of other auditors
• Review confirmations for unusual names

sections.title: Testing Related Party Transactions
sections.content:
**For identified transactions:**

• Verify authorization (Board approval often required)
• Examine contracts and terms
• Evaluate business purpose
• Compare to arm's-length terms
• Verify appropriate accounting
• Ensure complete disclosure

**Significant transactions may be significant risk!**

sections.title: Red Flags
sections.content:
**Indicators of undisclosed related parties:**

• Unusual terms or conditions
• Transactions lacking business purpose
• No apparent arm's-length
• Loans without repayment provisions
• Sales at unusual prices
• Guarantees without compensation
• Transactions outside normal course

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Disclosure Requirements
sections.content:
**What must be disclosed:**

• Nature of relationship
• Description of transaction(s)
• Dollar amounts
• Amounts due to/from

**What's NOT required:**
• Stating transactions were at arm's-length (unless auditor can verify!)

**Common fraud:** Company states "arm's-length" when terms aren't fair

sections.title: Previously Unknown Related Parties
sections.content:
**If discovered during audit:**

**1. Communicate** to engagement team
**2. Reassess** risk assessment
**3. Perform additional procedures:**
   • What other transactions exist?
   • Why wasn't it identified earlier?
   • Any other unknown relationships?
**4. Evaluate effect** on audit
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Family Businesses: A Recipe for Audit Headaches
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Auditing related party transactions, especially in family-owned businesses, can be a *pain*. Uncle Joe's 'generous' loan to the company? Yeah, that's probably not at arm's length. You gotta dig deep, ask the tough questions, and document *everything*. Don't be afraid to push back – your professional skepticism is your best friend here. Think of it like this: you're not accusing anyone of anything, you're just making sure everything is above board. And trust me, that's what everyone *should* want...right?

sections.title: Forgetting 'Significant Influence' Isn't Just Ownership
sections.content: A common mistake is focusing *solely* on direct ownership when identifying related parties. Students often miss the 'significant influence' part of the definition. Just because someone doesn't *own* 20% or more of a company doesn't mean they can't exert significant influence. For example, a major supplier who represents 70% of the company's revenue could be considered a related party due to their ability to influence pricing and other key decisions. Always consider the broader relationship and potential for influence, not just ownership percentages.

sections.title: Sarah's Surprise: The Undisclosed Lease
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, was reviewing lease agreements for a client. She noticed a lease for office space at a significantly lower rate than comparable properties in the area. Further investigation revealed the lessor was the CEO's brother-in-law. This related-party transaction was not disclosed in the footnotes. Sarah flagged this immediately, leading to a restatement of the financial statements to properly reflect the fair value of the lease and the related party relationship. Always be suspicious of unusually favorable terms.

sections.title: Related Parties: It's Everywhere, All at Once
sections.content: You'll encounter related-party transactions constantly in your career. From reviewing board minutes for potential conflicts of interest to scrutinizing expense reports for questionable reimbursements to family members, the need to identify and assess these transactions is ongoing. Understanding the GAAP definition and the potential risks is crucial. The PCAOB also emphasizes the importance of scrutinizing these areas. Master this now, and you'll be well-prepared for real-world audits and the scrutiny that comes with them.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Related parties include affiliates, owners, management, and family
Transactions may not be arm's-length—evaluate substance over form
Auditor must identify related parties and remain alert throughout audit
Test authorization, terms, business purpose, and disclosure
Red flags: unusual terms, no business purpose, non-arm's-length
Previously unknown related parties require reassessment
Disclosure of arm's-length only if auditor can verify

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following situations would most likely indicate the existence of previously undisclosed related party transactions?
options: Sales to a new customer at prices significantly above market rates.
A loan from the company to the CEO's brother-in-law, which is not disclosed in the financial statements.
Frequent purchases from a supplier located in a tax haven.
An unusually high volume of sales transactions occurring just before year-end.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is B. A loan to the CEO's brother-in-law, which is not disclosed, directly indicates a related party transaction that management is attempting to conceal. The other options could be indicators of other issues, but not necessarily related party transactions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what constitutes a related party and the requirement for disclosure.

reveal.question: List the three primary auditor responsibilities concerning related party transactions.
reveal.answer:
1. Obtain an understanding of related parties, their relationships, and transaction types.
2. Remain alert for related party transactions throughout the audit.
3. Evaluate the business purpose, accounting treatment, and disclosure of identified related party transactions.

rows.label: Pricing
values: Reflects fair market value, determined by supply and demand.
May not reflect fair market value; potentially influenced by the relationship.

rows.label: Disclosure
values: No specific disclosure requirement unless unusual terms exist.
Disclosure is required if the transaction is material, including the nature of the relationship and the terms of the transaction.

rows.label: Terms
values: Negotiated independently by each party.
Terms may be influenced by the relationship, potentially favoring one party.

title: Going Concern Evaluation
description: Evaluate management's assessment of the entity's ability to continue

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Going concern is a fundamental accounting assumption! If an entity may not survive, users need to know. Auditors must evaluate whether substantial doubt exists about the entity's ability to continue as a going concern—and the reporting implications are significant!

sections.title: Going Concern Assumption
sections.content:
**Basic principle:**
Financial statements are prepared assuming the entity will continue operations for the foreseeable future.

**If not a going concern:**
• Liquidation basis may be required
• Asset valuations affected
• Liability classifications change

**Evaluation period:** One year from F/S issuance date

sections.title: Conditions and Events
sections.content:
**Indicators of substantial doubt:**

**Financial:**
• Recurring operating losses
• Working capital deficiency
• Negative cash flows
• Defaulted loans or restructuring
• Unpaid dividends

**Operating:**
• Loss of key customer/supplier
• Labor difficulties
• Uninsured catastrophe
• Legal proceedings

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Going Concern Indicators
sections.content:
**"LOANS"** indicate trouble:

**L**osses—recurring operating losses
**O**ut of cash—negative cash flows
**A**greements violated—loan defaults
**N**egative working capital
**S**ources lost—key customers/suppliers

**When LOANS go bad, so does going concern!**

sections.title: Auditor Responsibilities
sections.content:
**Required procedures:**

**1. Evaluate conditions and events** that raise substantial doubt

**2. If substantial doubt raised, consider:**
   • Management's plans to mitigate
   • Likelihood plans will be implemented
   • Whether plans alleviate substantial doubt

**3. Conclude** and determine report impact

sections.title: Management's Plans
sections.content:
**Consider management's plans to address:**

• Sell assets
• Borrow money or restructure debt
• Reduce/delay expenditures
• Increase equity
• Obtain new customers

**Evaluate:**
• Are plans feasible?
• Is management capable of executing?
• What evidence supports likelihood of success?

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Alleviated vs. Remains
sections.content:
**Key distinction:**

**If management's plans ALLEVIATE substantial doubt:**
• May not need disclosure
• No emphasis of matter

**If substantial doubt REMAINS despite plans:**
• Disclosure required
• Emphasis of matter paragraph in report
• Still unmodified opinion (if disclosure adequate)

**The EOM is NOT a qualification!**

sections.title: Documentation
sections.content:
**Document:**

• Conditions/events identified
• Evaluation performed
• Management's plans and auditor's assessment
• Evidence obtained about likelihood of plans
• Conclusion reached
• Effect on audit report

**Important:** This can be a litigation target—document thoroughly!

sections.title: Subsequent Events Consideration
sections.content:
**After balance sheet date:**

• Continue evaluating through report date
• New information may:
  - Raise doubt previously not present
  - Alleviate doubt that existed

**Be alert:** Conditions change rapidly in distressed companies
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Going Concern: The 'Uh Oh' Moment
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. 'Going concern' is auditor code for 'Houston, we might have a problem.' You're basically asking, 'Is this company gonna be around next year?' If the answer is a shaky 'maybe,' you've got to dig deeper. I've seen companies with shiny offices but bleeding cash. Don't be fooled by appearances. Look for those red flags: consistent losses, unpaid bills piling up, and banks breathing down their necks. Trust your gut, and document everything!

sections.title: Confusing Temporary Dips with Terminal Decline
sections.content: Students often panic at the first sign of a financial hiccup, declaring 'not a going concern' prematurely. A single bad quarter doesn't necessarily mean the end. The key is to differentiate between a temporary setback and a fundamental, unsustainable decline. Consider the industry, management's plans for recovery, and the availability of alternative financing. Focus on the *likelihood* of survival, not just the presence of difficulties. Is it a bump in the road, or a cliff edge?

sections.title: Sarah's Sleepless Nights at Acme Corp
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, is reviewing Acme Corp's financials. They've shown consistent losses for three years and recently defaulted on a significant loan covenant. Management insists a new product launch will solve everything, but Sarah sees no concrete evidence to support this claim. Sarah needs to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence to evaluate management's plan, and if insufficient, consider the implications for the audit report. Ignoring this could lead to a major audit failure.

sections.title: Spotting the Signals in Real Life
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter going concern issues most often during preliminary analytical procedures. A sudden drop in profitability or a spike in debt levels should immediately raise your antenna. When reviewing contracts, pay close attention to default clauses and repayment schedules. Then, during substantive testing, scrutinize cash flow forecasts and management's plans to address financial difficulties. Mastering this skill early will make you a much more valuable and sought-after auditor.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Evaluate going concern for one year from F/S issuance date
Indicators: recurring losses, negative cash flows, loan defaults
Evaluate management's plans to address going concern doubt
If doubt alleviated—no disclosure may be required
If doubt remains—disclosure required and EOM paragraph
Inadequate disclosure = qualified or adverse opinion
Document conditions, plans, evidence, and conclusions

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following conditions or events would most likely raise a substantial doubt about an entity's ability to continue as a going concern?
options: A routine audit adjustment related to depreciation expense.
A significant decrease in the entity's customer base due to a competitor's aggressive marketing campaign.
An increase in the entity's sales revenue due to a new product launch.
A successful renegotiation of a long-term debt agreement with more favorable terms.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A significant decrease in the customer base threatens the entity's revenue stream and ability to generate profits, indicating potential going concern issues. The other options represent normal business operations or positive developments.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which scenario directly impacts the company's ability to generate revenue.

reveal.question: What is the typical evaluation period auditors use when assessing an entity's ability to continue as a going concern?
reveal.answer: One year from the date the financial statements are issued (or available to be issued).

rows.label: Management's Plans
values: Management operates the company as usual, without specific plans to address going concern issues.
Auditor MUST consider if management has plans to mitigate the going concern issue and assess the likelihood these plans will be effectively implemented.

rows.label: Auditor's Responsibility
values: Auditor performs normal audit procedures.
Auditor must gather evidence to support the feasibility and likelihood of successful implementation of management's plans.

title: Subsequent Events: Type I & Type II
description: Understand and audit events occurring after period end

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Events after year-end can significantly affect financial statements! Type I events require adjustment; Type II require disclosure. Knowing the difference—and the auditor's responsibilities during different periods—is essential for AUD success!

sections.title: Two Types of Subsequent Events
sections.content:
**Type I: Recognized Events**
• Provide evidence about conditions existing at B/S date
• ADJUST the financial statements

**Type II: Non-recognized Events**
• Conditions arose AFTER B/S date
• DISCLOSE only (no adjustment)

**Key question:** Did the condition EXIST at year-end?

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Type I vs Type II
sections.content:
**Type I = "It" existed**
• Condition existed at balance sheet date
• New info confirms what was already true
• ADJUST the statements

**Type II = "Two" = "New"**
• New condition after balance sheet date
• Didn't exist at year-end
• DISCLOSE only

**"I" = It existed → Adjust
"II" = New → Note disclosure**

sections.title: Type I Examples
sections.content:
**Adjust financial statements:**

• Customer bankruptcy (confirms A/R uncollectible at year-end)
• Settlement of litigation (clarifies year-end liability)
• Sale of inventory below cost (confirms NRV at year-end)
• Discovery of fraud affecting year-end balances

**Evidence of conditions that EXISTED at B/S date**

sections.title: Type II Examples
sections.content:
**Disclose only:**

• Sale of bonds or stock after year-end
• Business combination after year-end
• Catastrophic loss (fire, flood)
• New lawsuit filed
• Loss of major customer

**Conditions arose AFTER B/S date—no adjustment!**

sections.title: Three Key Periods
rows: No active duty, but if discovered, respond
No duty, but if discovered, may require action

sections.title: Required Subsequent Events Procedures
sections.content:
**Perform through report date:**

• Read latest interim F/S
• Inquire of management about:
  - Unusual adjustments
  - New commitments/contingencies
  - Changes in capital
  - Related party transactions
• Read Board minutes
• Obtain management representation letter
• Inquire of legal counsel

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Dual Dating
sections.content:
**If event discovered after report date:**

**Option 1: Redate entire report**
• Extends responsibility for ALL subsequent events
• Must update all procedures

**Option 2: Dual date**
• Original date PLUS new date for specific event
• "February 15, 20X2, except for Note X, as to which the date is March 1, 20X2"
• Responsibility extended ONLY for that matter

**Dual dating limits expanded responsibility!**

sections.title: After F/S Issued
sections.content:
**If subsequently discovered facts:**

**1. Discuss with management**
• Were F/S materially affected?
• What action will entity take?

**2. If entity won't act appropriately:**
• Notify governance
• Consider regulatory notification
• Consult legal counsel

**3. May need to notify users** not to rely on F/S
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Subsequent Events: Easier Said Than Done
sections.content: Alright, Subsequent Events. Sounds straightforward, right? Type I, Type II, got it. But let me tell you, spotting the *difference* in the heat of an audit is where things get tricky. You're sifting through mountains of documents, and suddenly, BAM! Customer X files for bankruptcy. Was this brewing *before* year-end, or is it a new issue? Dig deep. Ask questions. Don't just take the client's word for it. Your professional skepticism is your best friend here. Trust me, you'll thank me later when you're not explaining why you missed something obvious.

sections.title: The Timing Trap: When Did It REALLY Happen?
sections.content: A common mistake students make is focusing solely on the date of the *event* rather than the *condition* that caused it. For example, a major fire in a warehouse on January 15th might seem like a Type II event. However, if the fire was caused by faulty wiring known to be a problem since December, it could be argued that the condition existed at year-end, making it a Type I event requiring adjustment. Always ask: Did the underlying *condition* exist at the balance sheet date? If yes, it's likely a Type I.

sections.title: Sarah's Sticky Situation: The Lawsuit
sections.content: Sarah, a first-year auditor, is reviewing subsequent events for Acme Corp. She finds a lawsuit filed against Acme on February 20th, stemming from an incident in early January. The lawsuit alleges Acme's negligence caused significant environmental damage. While the lawsuit itself is after year-end, the incident leading to the lawsuit occurred before. This suggests a potential liability existed at year-end, requiring Sarah to investigate further and potentially adjust the financial statements for a probable and reasonably estimable loss.

sections.title: Real-World Impact: Protecting Your Opinion
sections.content: In practice, understanding subsequent events is crucial for forming an accurate audit opinion. Imagine you're auditing a construction company. After year-end, a major bridge they built collapses due to a design flaw. If this flaw existed at year-end, failing to identify and adjust for the potential liability could lead to a misleading audit report and potential legal repercussions for you and your firm. Thorough subsequent events review is a key risk mitigation activity.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Type I: Condition existed at B/S date → Adjust F/S
Type II: Condition arose after B/S date → Disclose only
Active procedures required through report date
Read minutes, inquire of management and legal counsel
Dual dating limits responsibility for late discoveries
After F/S issued: Discuss with management, may notify users
Key question: Did condition EXIST at balance sheet date?

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During the subsequent events period, an auditor discovers that a significant customer of the client declared bankruptcy due to events that occurred primarily after the balance sheet date. How should this subsequent event be treated in the financial statements?
options: Adjust the financial statements and disclose the event.
Adjust the financial statements only.
Disclose the event only.
Neither adjust nor disclose the event.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The customer bankruptcy arose from conditions occurring *after* the balance sheet date (primarily). Therefore, it's a Type II subsequent event requiring disclosure only.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider when the condition leading to bankruptcy primarily occurred.

sections.title: Type I vs. Type II Recall

reveal.question: What is the primary action required for a Type I subsequent event?
reveal.answer: Adjust the financial statements. Type I events provide evidence about conditions that existed at the balance sheet date.

sections.title: Type I vs. Type II Comparison

rows.label: Timing of Condition
values: Condition existed at B/S date
Condition arose after B/S date

rows.label: Financial Statement Action
values: Adjust financial statements
Disclose in footnotes

rows.label: Impact on Audit Report
values: May require revision of audit report if statements are adjusted
May require inclusion of explanatory paragraph if disclosure is extensive

knowledgeCheck.question: A company's fiscal year ends December 31. On February 15, Year 2, a lawsuit was filed against the company related to a product liability claim stemming from an incident that occurred in November of Year 1. The company's management believes the claim is without merit and that the likelihood of an unfavorable outcome is remote. How should this subsequent event be treated in the Year 1 financial statements?
options: Adjust the financial statements to reflect a probable loss.
Disclose the lawsuit in the footnotes to the financial statements.
Neither adjust nor disclose the lawsuit.
Adjust the financial statements to reflect a reasonably possible loss.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Since management believes the likelihood of an unfavorable outcome is remote, no adjustment or disclosure is required. The condition (lawsuit) arose after year-end and the pre-existing condition (product liability claim) has a remote likelihood of resulting in a loss.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider both the timing of the event (lawsuit) and the probability of a loss related to the underlying condition.

title: Using Internal Auditors' Work
description: Learn when and how to use the work of internal audit

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Internal auditors can provide valuable assistance—but you can't just accept their work blindly! Understanding how to evaluate internal audit and use their work appropriately can increase audit efficiency while maintaining quality. Know the boundaries!

sections.title: Internal vs External Auditors
sections.content:
**Key differences:**

**Internal Auditors:**
• Employees of the entity
• Report to management/governance
• Broader scope (operations, compliance, F/S)
• Part of entity's governance

**External Auditors:**
• Independent of entity
• Report to shareholders/users
• Focus on F/S assertions
• Sole responsibility for opinion

sections.title: Two Ways to Use Internal Audit
sections.content:
**1. Using the Work of Internal Auditors:**
• External auditor uses IA's completed work
• May reduce external audit procedures

**2. Direct Assistance:**
• IA performs work under external auditor's direction
• More supervision required
• Not permitted for significant risks or judgment areas

sections.title: Evaluating Internal Audit Function
sections.content:
**Assess:**

**Competence:**
• Education and experience
• Professional certifications
• Training programs

**Objectivity:**
• Reporting level (to audit committee best)
• Organizational status
• Policies on assignments

**Quality of work:**
• Adequate planning and supervision
• Appropriate documentation
• Reasonable conclusions

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Evaluating Internal Audit
sections.content:
**"COQ"** evaluates Internal Audit:

**C**ompetence—Are they qualified?
**O**bjectivity—Are they independent?
**Q**uality—Is their work good?

**Higher COQ = More reliance possible
Lower COQ = Less reliance (or none)**

**You want a COQ you can count on!**

sections.title: Determining Extent of Use
sections.content:
**Factors affecting how much to use:**

**Can use MORE when:**
• Higher objectivity (reports to audit committee)
• Greater competence
• Less subjectivity in the area
• Lower RMM for the area

**Can use LESS (or not at all) when:**
• Significant risks involved
• High subjectivity/judgment required
• Objectivity is compromised

sections.title: Limitations on Use
sections.content:
**Cannot use internal audit work (or direct assistance) for:**

• Making significant judgments
• Significant risks (unless supervised)
• Work related to their operating responsibilities
• Matters requiring significant audit judgment

**External auditor ALWAYS responsible for opinion!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Responsibility
sections.content:
**Using internal audit work does NOT:**

• Reduce external auditor's responsibility
• Allow reference to IA in report
• Eliminate need to test

**External auditor must:**
• Evaluate and test IA's work
• Make own conclusions
• Take full responsibility for opinion

**Never delegate responsibility—only work!**

sections.title: Direct Assistance
sections.content:
**When internal auditors work under direction:**

• External auditor supervises
• External auditor reviews work
• Prohibitions apply (significant risks, judgment)
• Some jurisdictions don't allow

**More supervision required than using completed work**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Internal Audit: Friend or Foe?
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. As an external auditor, you might initially see internal auditors as potential rivals. 'Are they trying to steal my job?' But seriously, a strong internal audit function is a HUGE asset. Think of them as your early warning system. If they're competent, they can save you tons of time testing controls. Just remember to assess their objectivity and competence first. Don't blindly trust them, but definitely leverage their work where appropriate. It's all about efficiency and reducing your own workload, right?

sections.title: Objectivity Blind Spot
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming all internal audit functions are created equal. Students often overlook the critical importance of assessing objectivity and competence before relying on their work. Just because a company *has* an internal audit department doesn't mean they're unbiased or skilled. Remember, internal auditors are employees. Their reporting lines and management influence can significantly impact their objectivity. You must thoroughly evaluate these factors and document your assessment. A poorly designed internal audit function can lead you down the wrong path, increasing audit risk.

sections.title: Sarah's Reliance Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, is auditing a large manufacturing company. The internal audit team has already tested inventory controls and found them to be effective. Sarah, eager to save time, decides to significantly reduce her own inventory testing. However, she failed to properly assess the internal audit team's competence or objectivity. Later, a material misstatement is discovered in inventory. The lesson? Always, always, always independently assess internal audit before relying on their work, regardless of how tempting it is to cut corners.

sections.title: Real-World Time Saver
sections.content: In practice, understanding how to use internal auditors' work can save you significant time and resources, especially on larger audits. Imagine auditing a multinational corporation. If the internal audit function is strong and has already tested controls in several locations, you can focus your efforts on higher-risk areas or locations where internal audit coverage is weaker. This targeted approach allows you to allocate your team's time more efficiently and provide a more effective audit. Plus, documenting your assessment of their work strengthens your audit file and defends your reliance decision.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Internal auditors can assist but external auditor retains responsibility
Two approaches: Use their work, or direct assistance
Evaluate competence, objectivity, and quality (COQ)
Can't use for significant risks or significant judgments
More objectivity and competence = more reliance possible
External auditor must evaluate and test IA work
No reference to internal audit in audit report

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Knowledge Check: Using Internal Auditor Work

knowledgeCheck.question: An external auditor is considering using the work performed by the internal audit function. Which of the following factors is LEAST likely to affect the external auditor's evaluation of the internal audit function's objectivity?
options: The organizational level to which the internal auditor reports.
The internal audit function's policies prohibiting audits of areas where internal auditors were recently assigned.
The internal audit function's access to the entity's records.
The internal audit function's participation in assessing risks across the organization.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The internal audit function's access to the entity's records primarily affects the quality of their work, not their objectivity. Objectivity is more closely tied to reporting structure, policies regarding prior assignments, and involvement in risk assessments as these factors influence potential bias.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what creates bias in the internal audit function.

reveal.question: True or False: An external auditor can utilize internal auditors for direct assistance related to assessing significant risks and making subjective judgments.
reveal.answer: False. Direct assistance from internal auditors is NOT permitted for tasks involving significant risks or requiring subjective judgments. The external auditor must maintain independence and expertise in these crucial areas.

rows.label: Reporting Responsibility
values: Management/Governance
Shareholders/Users

rows.label: Independence
values: Employees of the entity
Independent of the entity

rows.label: Scope of Work
values: Broader (operations, compliance, F/S)
Focus on F/S assertions

title: Component Auditors & Group Audits
description: Understand group audit responsibilities and component auditor oversight

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Most public companies have subsidiaries audited by other firms! Group auditors must oversee component auditors and take responsibility for the consolidated opinion. Understanding who does what—and who's responsible—is critical for these complex engagements!

sections.title: Key Terminology
sections.content:
**Group:** Parent plus components (subsidiaries, divisions, etc.)

**Component:** Entity or business unit with separate F/S

**Group Auditor:** Auditor of consolidated F/S (signs the report)

**Component Auditor:** Auditor of component F/S

**Group engagement partner:** Responsible for overall group audit

sections.title: Acceptance and Planning
sections.content:
**Before accepting group engagement:**

**Determine:**
• Can sufficient appropriate evidence be obtained?
• Is group auditor competent to take role?
• Can involvement with components be adequate?

**If answer is NO:** Don't accept!

sections.title: Understanding Components
sections.content:
**For each significant component:**

• Understand component and environment
• Identify component auditors
• Assess competence and independence
• Plan nature and extent of involvement

**Significant component = Individually financially significant OR significant risks

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Group Audit Duties
sections.content:
**"DISCO"** duties for group auditor:

**D**irect—set overall strategy
**I**nvolvement—participate in risk assessment
**S**upervise—monitor component work
**C**ommunicate—with component auditors
**O**verall conclusion—take responsibility

**Group auditor leads the DISCO!**

sections.title: Involvement with Component Auditor
sections.content:
**Procedures regarding component auditor:**

**1. Evaluate:**
   • Independence
   • Professional competence
   • Regulatory environment

**2. Communicate:**
   • Work to be performed
   • Ethical requirements
   • Materiality levels
   • Identified risks

**3. Review:**
   • Component auditor's work
   • Documentation (as needed)

sections.title: Work Required on Components
rows: Audit or specified procedures on risk areas

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Reference vs. No Reference
sections.content:
**Two approaches to reporting:**

**No reference (assumption of responsibility):**
• Group auditor takes full responsibility
• No mention of component auditor
• More common approach

**Reference (division of responsibility):**
• Permissible in non-issuer audits only
• Not a qualification
• Cannot refer if component audit inadequate

**PCAOB (issuers): No reference allowed!**

sections.title: Consolidation Process
sections.content:
**Group auditor must:**

• Evaluate appropriateness of consolidation
• Verify elimination entries
• Test intercompany transactions
• Evaluate uniform accounting policies
• Consider subsequent events for all components

**Cannot just add up—must verify consolidation!**

sections.title: Communication Requirements
sections.content:
**To component auditor:**
• Work required and timing
• Ethical requirements
• Materiality levels
• Risks requiring attention

**From component auditor:**
• Compliance with ethical requirements
• Work performed and conclusions
• Fraud indicators
• Significant deficiencies
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Group Audits: It's All About Control
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Group audits can feel like herding cats. You've got the parent company, then a bunch of component auditors all over the place. The key is understanding *control*. As the group auditor, you're responsible for the consolidated financials, but you can't just blindly trust what the component auditors are saying. You need to assess their competence, their independence, and the scope of their work. Think of it like this: you're the conductor of the orchestra, making sure everyone's playing the same tune, even if they're in different cities.

sections.title: Sufficiency vs. Appropriateness - Group Audit Edition
sections.content: Students often confuse 'sufficient appropriate audit evidence' at the *component* level with what's needed at the *group* level. Just because a component auditor gathered enough evidence for their component's financials doesn't automatically mean the group auditor has what they need for the consolidated statements. The materiality threshold is different! A misstatement that's immaterial to a component could be material to the group. Always consider the impact on the consolidated financials when evaluating component auditor evidence.

sections.title: Sarah's Scope Limitation Nightmare
sections.content: Sarah is the group engagement partner for a multinational corporation. One of the component auditors in a small foreign subsidiary refuses to allow Sarah's team access to the working papers due to local regulations. This is a scope limitation! Sarah now needs to assess the impact of this limitation on the group audit opinion. Can she obtain sufficient appropriate evidence through alternative procedures, or will she need to qualify or disclaim the opinion on the consolidated financial statements?

sections.title: Component Auditor Reliance: Real-World Application
sections.content: In practice, you'll encounter component auditor reliance *constantly* in large multinational audits. For example, imagine auditing a company with subsidiaries in 15 different countries. You'll likely rely on component auditors for the audits of those subsidiaries' financial statements. Therefore, it's critical to properly vet their competence, independence, and the quality of their work. Document your assessment thoroughly. Your firm's reputation, and the accuracy of the consolidated financials, depends on it. This includes reviewing their audit reports and performing procedures on their work.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Group auditor responsible for consolidated opinion
Must evaluate component auditor independence and competence
Significant components require audit or specified procedures
Two-way communication with component auditors required
Reference to component auditor: OK for non-issuers, not issuers
Group auditor must evaluate consolidation process
Cannot accept if insufficient evidence can be obtained

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Before accepting a group audit engagement, the group auditor should determine all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Whether sufficient appropriate audit evidence can be obtained regarding the components.
Whether the group engagement partner is competent to perform the group audit.
Whether the group auditor's involvement with the component auditors will be adequate.
Whether the component auditors are willing to accept the group auditor's direction.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: While the competence and independence of the component auditors is important, and the group auditor's involvement with them is critical, the component auditor's willingness to accept direction isn't a pre-acceptance requirement. The group auditor assesses the component auditor's work; they don't necessarily 'direct' them.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what the group auditor needs to know about the components *before* accepting the engagement. What risks are relevant at that stage?

reveal.question: Define 'significant component' in the context of a group audit.
reveal.answer: A component is considered significant if it is individually financially significant to the group OR if it presents significant risks of material misstatement to the group financial statements.

rows.label: Financial Statements Audited
values: Consolidated Financial Statements of the Group
Financial Statements of the Component

rows.label: Responsibility
values: Overall responsibility for the group audit opinion.
Audits a portion of the group and reports to the group auditor.

rows.label: Signs Audit Report
values: Yes, the group auditor signs the group audit report.
No, the component auditor does not sign the group audit report.

title: Using Specialists & Experts
description: Learn when and how to use the work of specialists

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Some audit areas require expertise auditors don't have—actuaries for pensions, appraisers for real estate, geologists for reserves. Knowing how to use specialists while maintaining audit responsibility is essential for complex engagements!

sections.title: Types of Specialists
sections.content:
**Management's Specialist:**
• Hired by management
• Creates information used in F/S
• Example: Actuary who calculates pension liability

**Auditor's Specialist:**
• Engaged by auditor
• Assists auditor with procedures
• Example: Valuation expert reviewing fair values

**Different evaluation requirements!**

sections.title: When to Use a Specialist
sections.content:
**Consider using when:**

• Matter requires specialized knowledge
• Area outside auditor's expertise
• High complexity or subjectivity

**Common areas:**
• Fair value measurements
• Actuarial valuations
• Reserves (oil, gas, minerals)
• Legal interpretations
• Engineering assessments

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Specialist Evaluation
sections.content:
**"CAR"** evaluates specialists:

**C**ompetence—Qualified for the work?
**A**ppropriate—Relevant expertise?
**R**elationship—Objectivity/independence?

**Before getting in the CAR, check the driver!**

sections.title: Evaluating Management's Specialist
sections.content:
**Evaluate:**

**1. Competence and capabilities:**
• Qualifications and certifications
• Experience with similar matters

**2. Objectivity:**
• Relationship with management
• Financial interests
• Controls over specialist's work

**3. Understand the work:**
• Nature of work performed
• Key assumptions used

sections.title: Using Management's Specialist Work
sections.content:
**Procedures:**

• Evaluate reasonableness of assumptions
• Evaluate appropriateness of methods
• Test data provided to specialist
• Consider if conclusions support F/S assertions

**Cannot accept work without evaluation!**

sections.title: Auditor's Specialist
sections.content:
**When auditor engages specialist:**

• Agree on scope and objectives
• Communicate responsibilities
• Evaluate specialist's work
• Determine effect on audit procedures

**Auditor remains responsible!**

**Note:** Auditor's specialist is NOT referred to in report

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Reference in Report
sections.content:
**General rule: Don't refer to specialists in report!**

**Reference suggests:**
• Divided responsibility (not allowed for auditor's specialist)
• Qualification of opinion

**Exception for management's specialist:**
• May refer when explaining basis for modified opinion
• Must indicate reference doesn't reduce auditor's responsibility

sections.title: Disagreement with Specialist
sections.content:
**If specialist's findings don't support assertions:**

**1. Discuss with management**
**2. Perform additional procedures:**
   • Additional specialist consultation
   • Expand audit procedures
**3. Consider effect on opinion**

**Cannot ignore specialist disagreement!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Specialists: Not Just For Rocket Science!
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest. When I first heard 'specialist,' I pictured physicists and rocket scientists. But in auditing? Think actuaries arguing about mortality rates or petroleum engineers drilling into reserve estimates. Seriously, these folks can make or break your audit, especially when dealing with complicated valuations. Don't be afraid to ask them the dumb questions - it's better to look ignorant than sign off on something you don't understand!

sections.title: Who's Specialist is it Anyway?
sections.content: A HUGE mistake is failing to distinguish between a management's specialist and an auditor's specialist. Students often think, 'Oh, a specialist is a specialist.' WRONG! Management's specialist is hired by the company being audited and their work is part of the financial statements. The Auditor's specialist is hired by YOU, the auditor, to HELP you evaluate the financial statements. If you mix them up, you'll struggle to properly assess objectivity and independence. Remember who's paying the piper!

sections.title: Fair Value Fiasco!
sections.content: John, a newly promoted audit manager, reviewed the fair value of a Level 3 asset (a complex derivative) using only management's documentation. He didn't engage a valuation specialist, even though he lacked the expertise to properly assess the assumptions. The next year, the derivative's value tanked, leading to a material misstatement. The lesson? If you lack the skills, bring in an expert! Don't let ego sink your audit.

sections.title: Actuaries & Pensions: A Common Audit Pairing
sections.content: You'll likely encounter actuaries in audits involving significant pension or other post-employment benefits (OPEB). These liabilities are massive, and the calculations are incredibly complex. In a typical audit, you'll be reviewing the actuary's assumptions (discount rate, mortality rates, etc.) and methodology. Don't just blindly accept their work; challenge their assumptions and ensure they're reasonable and supported by evidence. This is where an auditor's specialist, an actuarial expert hired by your firm, can be invaluable.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Two types: Management's specialist and auditor's specialist
Evaluate competence, appropriateness, and relationship (CAR)
Test reasonableness of specialist's assumptions and methods
Auditor remains responsible for opinion—can't delegate
Generally don't refer to specialist in report
If specialist findings contradict assertions—investigate
Common areas: Valuations, actuarial, reserves, legal

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During an audit of a manufacturing company, the auditor identifies a significant and complex warranty liability. Management engages an independent consultant with expertise in warranty claims to estimate the liability. Which of the following is the auditor's PRIMARY responsibility regarding this specialist?
options: To accept the specialist's findings without further evaluation, as the specialist is independent.
To evaluate the competence, capabilities, and objectivity of the specialist, and understand the specialist's work.
To engage a separate specialist to independently verify the management's specialist's findings.
To directly supervise the work of the management's specialist to ensure accuracy.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The auditor must evaluate the competence, capabilities, and objectivity of management's specialist, and understand the work performed to determine the reliability of the specialist's findings. While independence is a factor, it's not the only consideration. Directly supervising the specialist or automatically accepting the findings are not appropriate actions.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the auditor's overall responsibility for obtaining sufficient appropriate audit evidence.

reveal.question: An auditor engages a gemologist to assess the value of a jewelry collection held by a client. Is this considered a management's specialist or an auditor's specialist?
reveal.answer: This is an auditor's specialist. The auditor directly engaged the gemologist to assist with audit procedures and obtain audit evidence.

rows.label: Engagement
values: Engaged by management
Engaged by the auditor

rows.label: Role
values: Creates information used in financial statements
Assists the auditor in performing audit procedures

rows.label: Primary Evaluation Focus for Auditor
values: Competence, capabilities, objectivity, and understanding the work
Competence, capabilities, objectivity, and understanding the work

title: Substantive Testing: Revenue & Receivables
description: Master audit procedures for the revenue cycle

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Revenue is THE most scrutinized area on every audit! It's a presumed fraud risk and involves multiple assertions. Knowing the right procedures for revenue recognition and A/R testing is fundamental to audit success—and heavily tested on AUD!

sections.title: Why Revenue is High Risk
sections.content:
**Presumed fraud risk because:**

• Most common area for financial statement fraud
• Management pressure to meet targets
• Complex recognition criteria
• Multiple opportunities for manipulation
• ASC 606 adds complexity

**Key assertions:**
• Occurrence/Existence
• Cutoff
• Completeness
• Accuracy

sections.title: Revenue Audit Assertions
rows: Confirm with customers, vouch to shipping docs
Trace from shipping to sales journal

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Revenue Procedures
sections.content:
**"VOICE"** tests revenue:

**V**ouch sales to shipping documents
**O**ccurrence—confirm with customers
**I**nspect cutoff transactions
**C**ompleteness—trace from shipping
**E**valuate revenue recognition policies

**Give VOICE to revenue concerns!**

sections.title: Occurrence Testing
sections.content:
**Are recorded sales real?**

**Procedures:**
• Vouch sales to shipping documents
• Confirm with customers (positive confirmations)
• Examine customer purchase orders
• Review sales returns after year-end
• Test for unusual sales patterns

**Direction:** Book → Source (existence/occurrence)

sections.title: Cutoff Testing
sections.content:
**Are sales in the right period?**

**Procedures:**
• Select transactions around year-end
• Compare shipping date to recording date
• Examine shipping terms (FOB shipping vs. destination)
• Test sales returns after year-end
• Review credit memos near year-end

**Critical:** When does title pass?

sections.title: Accounts Receivable Testing
sections.content:
**Existence:**
• Confirm balances directly with customers
• Examine subsequent cash receipts

**Valuation:**
• Review aging of receivables
• Test allowance for doubtful accounts
• Examine subsequent collections
• Evaluate management's estimation process

**Rights:** Verify not pledged or factored

sections.title: A/R Confirmation Procedures
sections.content:
**Standard procedures:**

1. Select accounts (large, old, unusual)
2. Maintain control—send directly to customers
3. Receive responses directly
4. Investigate exceptions
5. Alternative procedures for non-responses:
   • Subsequent cash receipts
   • Shipping documents
   • Customer purchase orders

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Confirmation Direction
sections.content:
**Confirmations test EXISTENCE, not completeness!**

**Why?** You confirm what's recorded—can't confirm what isn't.

**For completeness, trace from:**
• Shipping documents → Sales journal
• Subsequent cash receipts → A/R

**Test FROM source documents TO books for completeness!**

sections.title: Allowance for Doubtful Accounts
sections.content:
**Testing the allowance:**

• Review aging reliability
• Test subsequent collections
• Evaluate historical loss rates
• Consider current economic conditions
• Test management's estimation process
• Compare to prior year and industry

**Valuation assertion = Is NRV reasonable?**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Revenue Recognition: The Wild West of Auditing
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Revenue recognition is where companies get creative (or, you know, fraudulent). Think of it as the Wild West of auditing. Management's under pressure to hit those targets, and ASC 606? It's like they gave them a whole new set of loopholes to explore. So, buckle up, because you're not just checking boxes here. You're playing detective. Trust your gut, and don't be afraid to ask the tough questions. A healthy dose of skepticism goes a long way when auditing revenue!

sections.title: Confusing Occurrence with Completeness in Sales
sections.content: Students often confuse occurrence (did the sale REALLY happen?) with completeness (are ALL the sales recorded?). Vouching sales invoices to shipping documents tests OCCURRENCE. We're ensuring a recorded sale actually occurred. On the other hand, tracing shipping documents to sales invoices tests COMPLETENESS. We're ensuring all shipments were recorded as sales. Remember, occurrence goes from journal to source document; completeness goes from source document to journal.

sections.title: The Case of the Missing Purchase Orders
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, is auditing MegaCorp. She's vouching sales transactions and notices that 20% of the invoices lack supporting customer purchase orders. This absence of purchase orders raises a red flag regarding the OCCURRENCE assertion. Without independent verification from the customer, Sarah needs to expand her testing. She should consider confirming sales directly with customers and performing alternative procedures to verify the legitimacy of these sales.

sections.title: Confirmations: Your Best Friend in Revenue Audits
sections.content: In practice, you'll find yourself sending out confirmations ALL the time when auditing revenue and receivables. It's a primary way to test the EXISTENCE of accounts receivable and, indirectly, the OCCURRENCE of sales. Positive confirmations, where the customer must respond, are your best bet for high-risk accounts or when you suspect potential issues. Getting that direct confirmation from the customer is invaluable. Don't skimp on this step – it can save you from missing a material misstatement.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Revenue is presumed fraud risk—high scrutiny required
Key assertions: Occurrence, cutoff, completeness, accuracy
Confirm A/R to test existence; trace from shipping for completeness
Cutoff testing focuses on transactions around year-end
Test shipping terms (FOB) to determine proper period
Evaluate allowance for doubtful accounts carefully
Confirmations test existence, NOT completeness

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following factors most significantly contributes to the inherent fraud risk associated with revenue recognition?
options: The relatively straightforward application of ASC 606.
Management's incentive to meet earnings targets, potentially leading to premature or fictitious revenue recognition.
The limited number of opportunities for manipulating sales transactions.
The infrequency with which revenue is a material component of financial statements.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Management's pressure to meet earnings targets creates a significant incentive to manipulate revenue, making it a high-risk area for fraud. While ASC 606 provides guidance, its complexity can also be exploited. Revenue is almost always a material component.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the motivations of management and the potential impact on the financial statements.

reveal.question: When performing substantive testing of revenue, what is the primary assertion being addressed when vouching sales transactions to shipping documents?
reveal.answer: Occurrence. Vouching from the sales journal to shipping documents helps verify that recorded sales actually occurred and were not fictitious.

knowledgeCheck.question: An auditor is concerned about the completeness assertion for revenue. Which of the following procedures would be MOST effective in addressing this concern?
options: Vouching sales invoices to shipping documents.
Tracing shipping documents to sales invoices.
Confirming accounts receivable with customers.
Reviewing sales returns after year-end.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Tracing shipping documents to sales invoices helps ensure that all shipments have been recorded as sales. Vouching addresses occurrence. Confirming A/R addresses existence/valuation. Sales returns are more related to accuracy/cutoff.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about which direction of testing would identify unrecorded transactions.

title: Substantive Testing: Inventory & PP&E
description: Learn key procedures for inventory observation and fixed assets

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Inventory is often the LARGEST current asset and most vulnerable to manipulation! PP&E testing involves both existence and valuation considerations. Understanding inventory observation procedures and fixed asset testing is essential for AUD!

sections.title: Inventory Audit Assertions
sections.content:
**Key assertions:**

**Existence:** Is inventory actually there?
**Completeness:** Is all inventory counted?
**Valuation:** At lower of cost or NRV?
**Rights:** Does entity own it?
**Accuracy:** Are quantities correct?

**Inventory observation is REQUIRED when inventory is material!**

sections.title: Inventory Observation
sections.content:
**Required procedures:**

**1. Attend physical count:**
• Evaluate client's count instructions
• Observe client personnel counting
• Make test counts
• Control count tags

**2. Document:**
• Test counts performed
• Tag numbers used
• Cutoff information

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Inventory Observation
sections.content:
**"COUNT"** inventory properly:

**C**ontrol the tags (no manipulation)
**O**bserve client counting
**U**se test counts (verify accuracy)
**N**ote cutoff information
**T**race to final inventory listing

**You must COUNT on inventory being correct!**

sections.title: Before, During, and After
rows: Review count instructions, identify locations, staff the observation
Observe procedures, make test counts, control tags, note cutoff
Trace test counts to final listing, perform cutoff procedures

sections.title: Inventory Cutoff
sections.content:
**Critical procedures:**

• Record last receiving document number
• Record last shipping document number
• Verify items in transit properly included/excluded
• Test transactions around year-end

**Shipping terms matter:**
• FOB shipping point: Title passes at shipment
• FOB destination: Title passes at receipt

sections.title: Inventory Valuation
sections.content:
**Testing lower of cost or NRV:**

• Test costing method (FIFO, average)
• Verify cost accumulation
• Review obsolete/slow-moving items
• Test selling prices for NRV
• Evaluate inventory reserves
• Consider market conditions

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Inventory Not Observed
sections.content:
**If auditor cannot observe inventory count:**

**Options:**
1. Observe count at alternative date, roll forward/back
2. Perform alternative procedures (if count already taken)

**If neither possible and inventory is MATERIAL:**
• Scope limitation
• Qualified opinion OR disclaimer

**Cannot skip inventory observation when material!**

sections.title: PP&E Testing
sections.content:
**Key procedures:**

**Existence:**
• Physical inspection of major additions
• Examine invoices and titles

**Completeness:**
• Trace from inspection to books
• Review lease commitments

**Valuation:**
• Test depreciation calculations
• Evaluate impairment indicators

**Rights:**
• Examine titles, deeds
• Search for liens

sections.title: PP&E Additions and Disposals
sections.content:
**Additions:**
• Vouch to supporting documentation
• Verify proper capitalization vs. expense
• Test classification
• Inspect major additions

**Disposals:**
• Verify authorization
• Recalculate gain/loss
• Confirm removal from records
• Verify no continued depreciation
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Inventory Counts: More Than Just Counting Beans
sections.content: Alright, future CPAs, let's be real. Inventory observation? Sounds boring, right? But trust me, it's crucial. I’ve seen so many shady inventory schemes in my career. Management might 'misplace' a few thousand units or conveniently 'find' some extra stock right before year-end. Your job is to be skeptical. Don't just blindly accept their count. Make those test counts, check those tags, and trust your gut. If something feels off, dig deeper! Think of yourself as a detective, not just a bean counter.

sections.title: Missing the Forest for the Trees: Obsolete Inventory
sections.content: A common mistake is focusing *solely* on the physical count and missing the valuation assertion. Students often correctly count the inventory but fail to assess whether it's obsolete. Imagine a tech company holding onto outdated circuit boards. They might exist (existence), and the count might be accurate (accuracy), but if they're unsellable, they're not valued correctly. Remember to consider market conditions, obsolescence, and net realizable value when evaluating inventory. Look for dust, outdated models, and slow-moving items. Ask management about obsolescence policies!

sections.title: Sarah's Tag Trouble: A Case of Missing Tags
sections.content: Sarah, a staff auditor, noticed a significant number of unattached inventory tags scattered around the warehouse during an inventory count at a manufacturing plant. The client's explanation was 'just a few fell off.' Sarah, remembering her AUD training, knew this could indicate a manipulation of the count. She immediately informed the senior auditor, who then expanded the test counts and investigated the tag control procedures, ultimately uncovering a scheme to overstate inventory. Always be alert!

sections.title: Real-World Relevance: Inventory & Loan Covenants
sections.content: In practice, understanding inventory and PP&E is critical because they often directly impact a company's compliance with loan covenants. Banks often set minimum working capital requirements, which heavily rely on accurate inventory valuation. Overstating inventory can make a company appear compliant when it's not, leading to serious consequences when the bank discovers the truth. Mastering these substantive tests will not only help you pass the AUD exam, but also safeguard you from making costly errors in your career.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Inventory observation is REQUIRED when inventory is material
Control tags, observe counting, make test counts
Document cutoff information (last shipping/receiving docs)
If can't observe: alternative date or scope limitation
Test inventory valuation at lower of cost or NRV
PP&E: Test additions, disposals, depreciation, impairment
Verify ownership rights (titles, liens)

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During an inventory observation, an auditor selects several items from the warehouse floor and traces them to the client's inventory listing. Which audit assertion is the auditor primarily testing?
options: Existence
Completeness
Valuation
Rights and Obligations
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Tracing from the warehouse floor to the inventory listing tests whether items physically present are also included in the inventory records, which directly addresses the completeness assertion. Existence would involve tracing from the inventory listing to the warehouse floor.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the direction of testing. Are you starting with the physical inventory or the inventory records?

reveal.question: List three required procedures that an auditor must perform during an inventory observation.
reveal.answer:
1. Evaluate the client's count instructions.
2. Observe client personnel counting inventory.
3. Make test counts of inventory items.
4. Control count tags used during the physical inventory count.
5. Document test counts performed, tag numbers used and cutoff information

knowledgeCheck.question: An auditor is concerned about the accuracy of inventory cutoff procedures at year-end. Which of the following procedures would be most effective in addressing this concern?
options: Observing the client's physical inventory count.
Reviewing the client's inventory obsolescence policy.
Examining shipping and receiving documents for several days before and after year-end.
Testing the client's inventory pricing methods.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Examining shipping and receiving documents around year-end helps the auditor determine if goods were properly included or excluded from inventory based on when title transferred. This directly tests the accuracy of the cutoff. The other options address different aspects of inventory but not the cutoff.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the timing of when inventory ownership transfers and how that impacts the accounting records.

title: Unmodified Audit Opinion
description: Understand the standard unmodified audit report

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: The unmodified opinion is the "clean" opinion—what everyone wants! Understanding its requirements and report elements is fundamental. You must know what conditions allow an unmodified opinion and recognize when modifications are needed!

sections.title: Conditions for Unmodified Opinion
sections.content:
**Unmodified opinion when:**

• F/S prepared in accordance with applicable framework (GAAP)
• No material misstatements identified
• Sufficient appropriate evidence obtained
• Adequate disclosures
• No scope limitations

**The "clean" opinion—F/S are fairly stated!**

sections.title: Report Elements (Non-Issuer)
sections.content:
**Required sections:**

1. **Title:** "Independent Auditor's Report"
2. **Addressee:** Usually shareholders/governance
3. **Opinion paragraph** (first!)
4. **Basis for Opinion paragraph**
5. **Responsibilities of Management**
6. **Auditor's Responsibilities**
7. **Auditor's signature**
8. **City and state** of auditor
9. **Date** of report

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Report Order
sections.content:
**"OBM-AR"** report structure:

**O**pinion (comes FIRST now!)
**B**asis for Opinion
**M**anagement's Responsibilities
**A**uditor's Responsibilities
**R**est (signature, date, location)

**Opinion upfront—what users want to know!**

sections.title: Opinion Paragraph Wording
sections.content:
**Standard language:**

"In our opinion, the financial statements present fairly, in all material respects, the financial position of [Company] as of [date], and the results of its operations and its cash flows for the year then ended in accordance with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America."

sections.title: Basis for Opinion Paragraph
sections.content:
**Includes:**

• Statement that audit was conducted in accordance with GAAS
• Reference to auditor's responsibilities section
• Statement of independence
• Statement that evidence is sufficient and appropriate

**Confirms HOW opinion was reached**

sections.title: Management Responsibilities
sections.content:
**Management responsible for:**

• Preparation of F/S in accordance with GAAP
• Internal control necessary for F/S preparation
• Evaluating going concern
• Disclosing relevant matters

**Critical:** Opinion addresses F/S, not controls (unless integrated)

sections.title: Auditor Responsibilities
sections.content:
**Auditor responsible for:**

• Obtaining reasonable assurance (not absolute!)
• Exercising professional judgment
• Maintaining professional skepticism
• Evaluating accounting policies and estimates
• Assessing risk of material misstatement
• Concluding on going concern
• Communicating with governance

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: PCAOB vs AICPA
sections.content:
**Key differences:**

**PCAOB (Issuers):**
• Critical Audit Matters required
• Integrated audit (F/S + IC)
• More detailed auditor tenure disclosure
• Specific report format

**AICPA (Non-Issuers):**
• Key Audit Matters optional
• IC report separate (if engaged)
• Simpler format

**Know which standards apply!**

sections.title: Report Dating
sections.content:
**Date = Day audit evidence is complete**

**Cannot be earlier than:**
