• Date sufficient evidence obtained
• Date F/S approved by management
• Date of management rep letter

**Auditor responsible for subsequent events through report date**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Clean Opinions: Easier Said Than Done
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. 'Unmodified opinion' sounds simple, right? Like, 'everything's perfect!' But in practice? It's rare. I've been in this game for 10 years, and I've only signed off on maybe 5 completely spotless audits. There's *always* something you have to dig into. So don't get lulled into a false sense of security. A clean opinion means you've done your due diligence to *prove* everything's fairly stated, not just assuming it is. Prepare for a deep dive!

sections.title: Assuming Immateriality = Unmodified
sections.content: A frequent error is thinking that *minor* errors automatically mean an unmodified opinion. Not quite! While immaterial misstatements *individually* might not warrant modification, their *aggregate* effect could be material. For example, three small overstatements adding up to $75,000 might seem insignificant for a $10 million company. However, if that $75,000 flips a key ratio from healthy to concerning, it could be material. Always consider the big picture and the qualitative factors.

sections.title: Sarah's Disclosure Dilemma
sections.content: Sarah, a first-year auditor, found a related-party transaction that wasn't explicitly illegal, but lacked sufficient disclosure in the footnotes. The transaction itself didn't materially misstate the numbers. However, because of the lack of adequate disclosure, Sarah knew the financial statements did not fully comply with GAAP. This meant that even though the numbers were correct, the opinion couldn't be unmodified. Adequate disclosure is key!

sections.title: The Opinion Paragraph: Your First Impression
sections.content: In the real world, the opinion paragraph is what everyone looks at first. Investors, lenders, even other auditors glancing at prior-year statements. It's the headline. When drafting, use precise language. Don't just say the financials are 'good'. State clearly and unequivocally that they are 'presented fairly, in all material respects, in accordance with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America.' Nail that wording down. It's your signature, and your reputation, on the line.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Unmodified opinion = F/S fairly stated in all material respects
Opinion paragraph comes FIRST in current format
Reasonable assurance, not absolute assurance
Management responsible for F/S and controls
Auditor responsible for opinion on F/S
Report date = when evidence is sufficient
PCAOB and AICPA reports have some differences

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following conditions would prevent an auditor from issuing an unmodified opinion on a non-issuer's financial statements?
options: The financial statements are prepared in accordance with GAAP, and the auditor obtained sufficient appropriate evidence.
The auditor's report is titled 'Independent Auditor's Report' and is addressed to the company's shareholders.
A scope limitation exists, preventing the auditor from obtaining sufficient appropriate evidence regarding a specific account balance.
Management acknowledges their responsibility for the fair presentation of the financial statements.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A scope limitation restricts the auditor's ability to gather sufficient evidence, preventing them from forming a complete opinion. This necessitates a modified opinion (qualified or disclaimer). The other options are conditions that support an unmodified opinion.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about what is essential for an auditor to form a complete and reliable opinion.

reveal.question: Fill in the blank: 'In our opinion, the financial statements present _____, in all _____ respects, the financial position of [Company] as of [date], and the results of its operations and its cash flows for the year then ended in accordance with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America.'
reveal.answer: fairly, material

sections.title: Comparison: Key Report Elements for Non-Issuers

rows.label: Title
values: Independent Auditor's Report
Identifies the report as being from an independent auditor.

rows.label: Addressee
values: Shareholders/Governance
Specifies to whom the report is directed.

rows.label: Opinion Paragraph
values: First paragraph
States the auditor's opinion on whether the financial statements are fairly presented.

rows.label: Basis for Opinion
values: Follows Opinion
Explains the basis for the auditor's opinion, including reference to auditing standards.

rows.label: Management's Responsibility
values: Discussed
Outlines management's responsibilities for the financial statements.

rows.label: Auditor's Responsibility
values: Discussed
Outlines the auditor's responsibilities in conducting the audit.

rows.label: Signature
values: Auditor's
Indicates who performed the audit.

rows.label: City/State
values: Included
The location where the auditor practices

title: Modified Opinions: Qualified, Adverse, Disclaimer
description: Master the three types of modified audit opinions

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Not all audits end with a clean opinion! Knowing when to qualify, disclaim, or issue an adverse opinion is crucial. The decision depends on whether the issue is material, pervasive, and whether it's a misstatement or inability to obtain evidence!

sections.title: Two Causes of Modification
sections.content:
**1. Material Misstatement:**
• F/S do not comply with framework (GAAP departure)
• Inadequate disclosures

**2. Inability to Obtain Evidence:**
• Scope limitation (by client or circumstances)
• Cannot determine if F/S are correct

**These drive different opinions!**

sections.title: Materiality Levels
sections.content:
**Material but NOT pervasive:**
• Affects specific accounts/areas
• Does not affect overall understanding
• Isolated to particular elements

**Material AND pervasive:**
• Affects many accounts or elements
• Represents substantial proportion of F/S
• Is fundamental to user understanding
• Disclosures are pervasively inadequate

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Opinion Selection
sections.content:
**"SAD"** for modified opinions:

**S** = Scope limitation → Qualified or Disclaimer
**A** = GAAP departure → Qualified or Adverse
**D** = Determine pervasiveness

**Pervasive? Go to the extreme!**
• Scope → Disclaimer (can't form opinion)
• GAAP → Adverse (opinion is bad)

sections.title: Qualified Opinion
sections.content:
**"Except for..."**

**Use when:**
• Material misstatement, but NOT pervasive, OR
• Unable to obtain evidence, but NOT pervasive

**Report includes:**
• Basis for Qualified Opinion paragraph
• Describes the issue and effect
• Opinion states "except for"

**Still useful opinion—just with exception**

sections.title: Adverse Opinion
sections.content:
**"F/S are NOT fairly stated"**

**Use when:**
• Material misstatement
• AND pervasive effect

**Report includes:**
• Basis for Adverse Opinion paragraph
• Describes departure and effects
• Opinion states F/S do NOT present fairly

**Rare—management usually fixes before this!**

sections.title: Disclaimer of Opinion
sections.content:
**"We cannot express an opinion"**

**Use when:**
• Unable to obtain sufficient evidence
• AND pervasive effect

**Report includes:**
• Basis for Disclaimer paragraph
• Explains why evidence couldn't be obtained
• States auditor does NOT express opinion

**Auditor couldn't do the job!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Cannot Mix and Match
sections.content:
**Each issue gets ONE opinion:**

• Can't have qualified AND adverse
• Different issues may require different treatments

**When multiple issues:**
• Use the MORE severe opinion
• Adverse "trumps" qualified for GAAP issues
• Disclaimer "trumps" qualified for scope issues

**One report, one opinion (the worst one)!**

sections.title: Report Modifications
sections.content:
**For modified opinions:**

**Change:**
• Opinion paragraph title
• Add "Basis for [Modified] Opinion" paragraph
• Explain the matter and effects

**Basis paragraph goes AFTER Opinion paragraph (before for disclaimer)**

**Quantify effects when practicable**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Modified Opinions: It's All About the Impact!
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Modified opinions aren't just textbook terms. They're about judging how badly a mistake screws up the whole financial picture. Think of it like this: a typo on page 5 is a qualified opinion waiting to happen. But a massive fraud that makes the entire income statement unreliable? That's adverse territory. Scope limitations? If the client refuses you access to vital records, like inventory counts, you might be forced to disclaim an opinion altogether. Don't panic, just assess the damage!

sections.title: Confusing 'Material' with 'Pervasive'
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking that if something is material, it's automatically pervasive. Not true! Materiality is about the size or nature of the misstatement. Pervasiveness is about how widespread the effect is. For example, a $50,000 error in accounts receivable for a billion-dollar company might be material (depending on materiality thresholds), but it's probably NOT pervasive. It's isolated to one account. However, a $50,000 error that affects revenue recognition across multiple segments and years could be both material AND pervasive.

sections.title: Sarah's Scope Limitation Nightmare
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, is auditing a small manufacturing company. During inventory observation, a fire alarm forces everyone to evacuate before she can complete her test counts. Management refuses to reschedule the observation, citing production deadlines. Because Sarah is unable to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence relating to inventory, a major component of the balance sheet, she'll likely have to issue a qualified opinion, or potentially a disclaimer, due to the scope limitation.

sections.title: Spotting Scope Limitations in Real Life
sections.content: In practice, scope limitations crop up more often than you'd think. Clients sometimes try to limit your access to information, either intentionally or unintentionally. Missing documentation, system outages, or uncooperative personnel can all create scope limitations. When you encounter one, document it *immediately*. Discuss it with your engagement partner, and thoroughly evaluate the potential impact on your opinion. Remember, protecting the integrity of the audit is paramount, even if it means delivering bad news to the client.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Two causes: Misstatement or inability to obtain evidence
Two levels: Material only vs. material AND pervasive
Qualified: "Except for" (material, not pervasive)
Adverse: F/S not fairly stated (GAAP + pervasive)
Disclaimer: Can't express opinion (scope + pervasive)
Use the more severe opinion when multiple issues
Basis for opinion paragraph explains the modification

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: During the audit of XYZ Company, the auditor discovers that management has not properly disclosed a significant related party transaction. This omission is deemed material, but not pervasive, to the financial statements. Which of the following best describes the situation?
options: Scope limitation leading to a disclaimer of opinion.
Material misstatement leading to a qualified opinion.
Scope limitation leading to a qualified opinion.
Material misstatement leading to an adverse opinion.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is B. A material, but not pervasive, misstatement due to a GAAP departure (inadequate disclosure) results in a qualified opinion. A scope limitation would arise from an inability to obtain sufficient appropriate audit evidence. An adverse opinion is appropriate when the misstatement is material AND pervasive.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the cause of the modification: is it a GAAP departure or a limitation on gathering evidence?

reveal.question: Define 'pervasive' in the context of financial statement misstatements.
reveal.answer: A misstatement is considered 'pervasive' if it: (1) affects many accounts or elements in the financial statements; (2) represents a substantial portion of the financial statements; or (3) is fundamental to users' understanding of the financial statements.

rows.label: Materiality
values: Material, but not pervasive
Material AND pervasive

rows.label: Nature of Misstatement
values: Specific accounts/areas affected; does not affect overall understanding
Affects many accounts; substantial proportion of F/S; fundamental to user understanding

rows.label: Auditor Conclusion
values: F/S are presented fairly in all *material* respects, except for the matter(s) described
F/S are NOT presented fairly in conformity with applicable accounting framework

title: Emphasis of Matter & Other Matter Paragraphs
description: Understand when to add informational paragraphs to the audit report

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Sometimes you need to highlight important information without modifying your opinion! Emphasis of Matter and Other Matter paragraphs draw attention to issues already disclosed or explain audit matters. Knowing when to use each is tested frequently on AUD!

sections.title: Emphasis of Matter (EOM)
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Draw attention to a matter properly disclosed in F/S that is fundamental to users' understanding.

**Key features:**
• Matter IS disclosed in F/S
• Just highlighting it
• Does NOT modify opinion
• NOT a qualification

**Title: "Emphasis of Matter" (or descriptive)**

sections.title: When EOM Required
sections.content:
**Required EOM:**

• Substantial doubt about going concern (properly disclosed)
• Change in accounting principle with material effect
• Major catastrophe significantly affecting F/S
• Significant related party transactions

**Key:** Appropriately disclosed but fundamental to understanding

sections.title: EOM Paragraph Contents
sections.content:
**Must include:**

1. Clear reference to matter being emphasized
2. Where it's disclosed in F/S (note reference)
3. Statement that opinion is NOT modified

**Placement:** After Basis for Opinion, before Auditor's Responsibilities

**Example:** "As discussed in Note X, the Company has substantial doubt about its ability to continue as a going concern. Our opinion is not modified with respect to this matter."

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: EOM vs OM
sections.content:
**"EOM = Eyes on Material"**
• Points to F/S disclosures
• "Look at this important note!"

**"OM = Other Matters"**
• About the audit itself
• "Here's something about our work"

**EOM = F/S focus
OM = Audit focus**

sections.title: Other Matter (OM) Paragraph
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Communicate matters relevant to users' understanding of the audit, auditor's responsibilities, or auditor's report.

**NOT disclosed in F/S—about the audit itself**

**Examples:**
• Prior year audited by another auditor
• Restricting report distribution
• Required supplementary information matters

sections.title: Common OM Situations
sections.content:
**Other Matter paragraphs for:**

• Prior year reviewed, not audited
• Prior year audited by predecessor auditor
• Report on both F/S and supplementary info
• Restriction on use of report
• Separate report on ICFR
• Supplementary information in document

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: EOM is NOT Qualification
sections.content:
**Common misconception:**

"EOM means something is wrong."

**WRONG!** EOM:
• Does NOT modify opinion
• Is NOT a qualification
• Matter is PROPERLY disclosed
• Opinion remains unmodified

**If disclosure were inadequate → Qualified opinion, not EOM!**

sections.title: Placement in Report
sections.content:
**Emphasis of Matter:**
• After Basis for Opinion
• Before Auditor's Responsibilities

**Other Matter:**
• Generally after Emphasis of Matter (if both)
• May be at end depending on nature

**Both come with clear headers indicating what they are**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: EOM: The 'Hey, Look Over Here!' Flag
sections.content: Okay, let's be real. Emphasis of Matter paragraphs? They're like waving a bright flag saying, 'Yo, stakeholders! Check this out!' It's not that the financials are wrong; it's that there's something super important you need to understand. Think of it as the auditor's way of saying, 'We've done our job, it's all good, but seriously, read footnote X about that $5 million lawsuit – it's kind of a big deal.' It's all about transparency, baby! Don't skip the footnotes!

sections.title: EOM vs. Qualification: Knowing the Difference
sections.content: A common mistake is thinking an Emphasis of Matter paragraph means the opinion is qualified. Huge no-no! EOMs are purely informational; they don't change the auditor's opinion. A qualified opinion means something's materially misstated or scope is limited. EOMs are used when everything is disclosed properly. If you find yourself wavering, ask yourself: 'Is the issue already disclosed in the financial statements? If yes, it might be EOM territory. If no, it's a qualification issue.'

sections.title: Sarah's Going Concern Quandary
sections.content: Sarah, a senior auditor, is reviewing the draft financials of a tech startup. The company discloses in its footnotes that it might not have enough cash to continue operations for the next year, but the disclosure is thorough. Sarah correctly decides to include an Emphasis of Matter paragraph highlighting the going concern uncertainty. This informs users of the financial statements about the critical risk the company faces without changing the clean audit opinion on the financial statements themselves.

sections.title: Spotting EOM Triggers in the Wild
sections.content: In practice, you'll often find yourself drafting EOM paragraphs when dealing with clients in industries prone to rapid change or unique risks. For example, a biotech firm might have an EOM about a major patent dispute, or a construction company about a large project delay due to weather. Knowing when to flag these events is critical. It shows you're not just checking boxes, but thinking critically about what matters most to the users of the financial statements.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: EOM highlights matters disclosed in F/S—fundamental to understanding
Other Matter covers audit-related information
Neither EOM nor OM modify the opinion
Required EOM: Going concern, accounting changes, catastrophes
EOM must reference where disclosed and state opinion not modified
OM covers prior period audited by others, restrictions, etc.
EOM is NOT a qualification—matter is properly disclosed

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: An auditor is expressing an unmodified opinion on a company's financial statements. The company has experienced a major fire in its main production facility, which is disclosed in the notes to the financial statements. Which of the following is the *most* appropriate action for the auditor?
options: Issue a qualified opinion due to the significant impact of the fire.
Include an emphasis-of-matter paragraph after the opinion paragraph to highlight the fire and its impact.
Include an emphasis-of-matter paragraph after the basis for opinion paragraph to highlight the fire and its impact.
Disclaim an opinion due to the uncertainty surrounding the fire's long-term effects.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An emphasis-of-matter paragraph is used to draw attention to a matter appropriately presented or disclosed in the financial statements that is fundamental to users' understanding. The paragraph is placed immediately after the basis for opinion paragraph. The fire, properly disclosed, warrants an EOM. It does not require a modification of the opinion.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the purpose of an emphasis-of-matter paragraph and when it's appropriate.

reveal.question: List three situations that *require* an Emphasis-of-Matter paragraph.
reveal.answer:
1. Substantial doubt about going concern (properly disclosed)
2. Change in accounting principle with a material effect
3. Major catastrophe significantly affecting the financial position

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is *not* required in an emphasis-of-matter paragraph?
options: A clear reference to the matter being emphasized.
A statement that the auditor's opinion is not modified.
The auditor's assessment of management's competence.
A reference to where the matter is disclosed in the financial statements.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An emphasis-of-matter paragraph must include a clear reference to the matter being emphasized, a statement that the auditor's opinion is not modified, and a reference to where the matter is disclosed in the financial statements. The auditor's assessment of management's competence is not a required element.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the core information the user *needs* to understand the highlighted issue.

title: Comparative Financial Statements
description: Understand reporting when prior periods are presented

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Most F/S present multiple years! Understanding how to report on comparatives—especially when opinions differ between periods or another auditor was involved—is essential. These scenarios are frequently tested on AUD!

sections.title: Comparative Reporting Basics
sections.content:
**When comparatives presented:**

• Auditor's opinion covers ALL periods presented
• Current auditor may not have audited prior periods
• Opinion may differ between periods

**Two approaches:**
1. Updating prior year opinion (same auditor)
2. Referring to predecessor auditor (different auditor)

sections.title: Updating Prior Opinion (Same Auditor)
sections.content:
**If same auditor for both periods:**

• Opinion covers both years
• May have different opinions by year
• Current events may change prior opinion

**Examples:**
• Prior qualified, now unmodified (issue resolved)
• Prior unmodified, now qualified (prior issue discovered)
• Both years have same opinion

sections.title: Different Prior Period Opinion
sections.content:
**If prior period had different opinion:**

**Add Other Matter paragraph:**
• Reference prior period opinion
• State type of modification and reason
• Indicate if matter resolved or continues

**Example language:** "In our report dated [date], we expressed a qualified opinion because of [reason]. The situation has been resolved and our current opinion is unmodified."

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Prior Period Reference
sections.content:
**"WRITE"** about prior opinions:

**W**hat was the prior opinion?
**R**eason for that opinion
**I**s it resolved now?
**T**ype of current opinion
**E**xplain the change

**Don't leave users in the dark about changes!**

sections.title: Predecessor Auditor Situations
sections.content:
**If prior period audited by another auditor:**

**Option 1: Predecessor reissues report**
• Predecessor's report included with current report
• Clear identification of who audited what

**Option 2: Current auditor refers to predecessor**
• Other Matter paragraph
• States prior year audited by others
• Type of opinion predecessor expressed

sections.title: Reference to Predecessor
sections.content:
**Other Matter paragraph includes:**

• Prior period F/S audited by another auditor
• Date of predecessor's report
• Type of opinion expressed
• Any modifications and reasons

**Current auditor does NOT take responsibility for predecessor's work!**

sections.title: Prior Period Not Audited
sections.content:
**If prior period was reviewed or not audited:**

**Other Matter paragraph:**
• States prior period was not audited
• States level of service (review, compilation, none)
• If reviewed: Limited assurance provided
• If compiled: No assurance provided

**Cannot be silent about unaudited comparatives!**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Restated Prior Period
sections.content:
**If prior period F/S are restated:**

**Current auditor must:**
• Audit restatement adjustments
• Update opinion on restated F/S
• Add explanatory language about restatement

**Predecessor involved?**
• May need to reissue report
• Or current auditor addresses in report

**Restatements require special attention!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Prior Year Opinions: A Pain in the Audit!
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, dealing with prior year opinions can be a real headache. You're knee-deep in the current year audit, and suddenly you have to unravel what happened last year? Ugh! The key is to be meticulous in reviewing the prior year's workpapers. Did the predecessor auditor flag anything significant? Has management addressed those issues? Don't just blindly accept the prior opinion. Your professional skepticism needs to be on high alert. Trust me, a little extra digging now can save you from a major embarrassment later.

sections.title: Forgetting to Update Prior Opinions!
sections.content: A common mistake is assuming that just because the prior year opinion was 'unmodified', it automatically stays that way. A significant event happening *after* the prior year's audit, but *before* the current year's audit report date, could change everything. For example, a major lawsuit settled unfavorably after the prior year's report date but related to the prior year. You MUST evaluate whether this affects the prior year's financial statements and whether an updated opinion is necessary. Failing to do so is a big no-no and could lead to a misstatement.

sections.title: Sarah's Surprise: A Predecessor's Departure
sections.content: Sarah is reviewing comparative financial statements for a new client, Acme Corp. The prior year audit was performed by a different firm. Sarah discovers the predecessor auditor's report included a 'going concern' paragraph. Sarah needs to determine if Acme Corp. has resolved the going concern issue. If not, she needs to decide if she needs to include an emphasis-of-matter paragraph in her current year's report, even if the current year financials don't independently raise going concern issues. Ignoring the predecessor's findings would be a mistake.

sections.title: Real-World Impact: Opinion Shopping
sections.content: In practice, understanding comparative financial statement reporting helps you identify potential 'opinion shopping'. A company might switch auditors after receiving a qualified opinion, hoping the new auditor will be more lenient. If you encounter this situation, be extra cautious. Scrutinize the prior year's workpapers and the reasons for the auditor change. Document your findings thoroughly. Your independence and objectivity are paramount. Remember, protecting the public interest is your primary responsibility, even if it means delivering bad news to a client.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Auditor's opinion covers all periods presented
Different opinions by period require explanatory language
Predecessor auditor: Either reissues report or is referenced
Other Matter paragraph explains prior period situations
Never remain silent about unaudited comparative periods
Restated prior periods require special procedures
Current auditor doesn't take responsibility for predecessor work

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: When comparative financial statements are presented, and the current auditor did not audit the prior period, what is the auditor's responsibility regarding the prior period's financial statements and the prior auditor's report?
options: The current auditor must audit the prior period's financial statements to issue an opinion on the comparative financial statements.
The current auditor should refer to the predecessor auditor's report in the current report, including the type of opinion expressed and the reasons for any modification.
The current auditor is not responsible for the prior period's financial statements or the predecessor auditor's report.
The current auditor must re-issue the predecessor auditor's report alongside the current report.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The current auditor refers to the predecessor auditor's report to ensure users are aware of the prior period's audit opinion. This includes the type of opinion and reason for any modification. The current auditor doesn't audit the prior period or re-issue the prior report.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the auditor's responsibility for periods they did not audit.

reveal.question: What information must be included in an Other Matter paragraph when the prior period's financial statements had a different opinion than the current period, and the same auditor performed both audits?
reveal.answer: The Other Matter paragraph should reference the prior period's opinion, state the type of modification and the reason for it, and indicate whether the matter that caused the modification has been resolved or continues to exist.

rows.label: Reason for Change
values: The matter that caused the prior qualification has been resolved.
A matter arose in the current period (or was discovered relating to the prior period) that requires qualification.

rows.label: Reporting
values: No specific mention of the prior qualification is needed in the current report, although professional judgment may dictate disclosure.
An Other Matter paragraph is required to explain the qualification in the current period and, if related, the prior period.

title: Key Audit Matters (KAM) / Critical Audit Matters (CAM)
description: Understand expanded audit reporting requirements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: KAM and CAM represent the biggest change to audit reporting in decades! They provide more insight into significant audit areas. PCAOB requires CAMs for issuers; AICPA has optional KAMs. Understanding what they are and how they differ is essential!

sections.title: What Are KAM/CAM?
sections.content:
**Key Audit Matters (KAM)** - International/AICPA:
• Matters of most significance in the audit
• Selected from matters communicated to governance
• Voluntary for non-issuers under AICPA

**Critical Audit Matters (CAM)** - PCAOB:
• Required for issuer audits
• Matters communicated to audit committee
• Involved especially challenging judgment or evidence

sections.title: CAM Requirements (PCAOB)
sections.content:
**CAM is a matter that:**

1. Was communicated (or required to be) to audit committee
2. Relates to material accounts or disclosures
3. Involved especially challenging, subjective, or complex auditor judgment

**All three criteria must be met!**

sections.title: CAM Determination Process
sections.content:
**Consider factors:**

• Degree of auditor judgment
• Significant risk areas
• Unusual transactions
• Degree of subjectivity in measurement
• Nature and extent of audit effort
• Difficulty obtaining evidence
• Significant events or transactions

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: CAM Criteria
sections.content:
**"CCC"** makes a CAM:

**C**ommunicated to audit committee
**C**oncerns material accounts
**C**hallenging judgment required

**All three C's must be present!**

sections.title: CAM Communication in Report
sections.content:
**For each CAM, describe:**

1. Principal considerations leading to determination
2. How the CAM was addressed in the audit
3. Reference to relevant F/S accounts or disclosures

**Placement:** Separate section titled "Critical Audit Matters"

**After Basis for Opinion section**

sections.title: What Cannot Be CAM
sections.content:
**CAM does NOT include:**

• Going concern (separate EOM required)
• Matters giving rise to modified opinion
• Matters that are routine or insignificant
• Matters not requiring significant judgment

**CAM is about the hardest, most judgment-intensive areas**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: KAM vs CAM
sections.content:
**Key differences:**

**CAM (PCAOB/Issuers):**
• Required for all public company audits
• Specific three-part criteria
• More detailed requirements

**KAM (AICPA/Non-Issuers):**
• Optional (voluntary)
• Similar concept but different criteria
• More judgment in selection

**Know which applies to your question!**

sections.title: No CAMs Identified
sections.content:
**If no CAMs exist (rare):**

• Still include CAM section in report
• State that auditor has determined there are no CAMs

**Unusual** but possible for very simple audits

**Don't just omit the section!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: KAM & CAM: Decoding the Alphabet Soup
sections.content: Okay, let's be honest, KAM and CAM sound like some sort of secret agent training program. But really, it's about making audit reports more useful. Think of KAMs as the auditor's 'greatest hits' from the audit – the stuff they really want to highlight for investors. CAMs, the PCAOB's version for public companies, are like the 'director's cut' – extra details on the trickiest parts. Don't get bogged down in the acronyms, focus on the 'why': better communication and transparency.

sections.title: KAM vs. CAM: Mixing Apples and Oranges
sections.content: A frequent mistake is thinking KAM and CAM are interchangeable. While they both aim to highlight significant audit matters, remember KAM is voluntary for non-issuers under AICPA standards, while CAM is required for issuer audits under PCAOB standards. Students often miss this distinction and assume all audits require both. Think of it this way: CAM is the stricter, PCAOB-mandated version specifically for public companies. Always check the audit client type to determine the reporting requirements.

sections.title: Sarah's CAM Conundrum: Revenue Recognition
sections.content: Sarah, a new senior auditor, is reviewing the draft audit report for a publicly traded software company. The engagement partner flagged revenue recognition as a potential CAM due to the complexity of recognizing revenue from long-term contracts. Sarah needs to determine if this revenue recognition issue was communicated to the audit committee, relates to material accounts, and involved significant auditor judgment. If all three criteria are met, it's likely a CAM that needs to be disclosed in the audit report.

sections.title: KAM/CAM: Making Your Audit Matter
sections.content: In practice, understanding KAM and CAM is crucial for effective audit planning and execution. When scoping an audit, you'll need to identify potential KAMs/CAMs early on. This will influence the nature, timing, and extent of your audit procedures. For example, if complex revenue recognition is a potential CAM, you'll need to design procedures to specifically address that risk. Documenting your consideration of KAMs/CAMs is essential for supporting your audit opinion and demonstrating due professional care.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: CAM required for issuers (PCAOB); KAM optional for non-issuers
CAM: Communicated to AC, material accounts, challenging judgment
Describe how CAM was addressed and reference F/S accounts
Going concern and modified opinion matters are NOT CAMs
Separate CAM section in report after Basis for Opinion
If no CAMs: Still include section stating none identified
CAMs provide transparency about audit's most challenging areas

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: KAM vs. CAM - Which is Which?

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding Key Audit Matters (KAM) and Critical Audit Matters (CAM)?
options: KAMs are required for all issuer audits under PCAOB standards.
CAMs are required for all audits under AICPA standards.
KAMs are selected from matters communicated to those charged with governance, while CAMs are selected from matters communicated to the audit committee.
CAMs are voluntary for non-issuers under AICPA standards.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is that KAMs are selected from matters communicated to those charged with governance, while CAMs are selected from matters communicated to the audit committee. KAMs are voluntary for non-issuers under AICPA, and CAMs are required for issuer audits under PCAOB.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which standard setter requires mandatory reporting of key audit findings and to whom those findings are communicated.

reveal.question: List the three criteria that MUST be met for a matter to be considered a Critical Audit Matter (CAM) under PCAOB standards.
reveal.answer:
1. Was communicated (or required to be) to the audit committee.
2. Relates to material accounts or disclosures.
3. Involved especially challenging, subjective, or complex auditor judgment.

sections.title: KAM vs. CAM: A Quick Comparison

rows.label: Standard Setter
values: AICPA (Voluntary for non-issuers)
PCAOB (Required for issuers)

rows.label: Communication Recipient
values: Those charged with governance
Audit Committee

rows.label: Mandatory/Voluntary
values: Voluntary for non-issuers under AICPA
Mandatory for issuer audits under PCAOB

title: Attestation: Examination Engagements
description: Understand examination engagements under the attestation standards

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Attestation engagements go beyond auditing financial statements! Examination engagements provide high-level assurance on subject matter like compliance, controls, or prospective information. Understanding attestation standards (AT-C) is essential for the AUD exam!

sections.title: What is Attestation?
sections.content:
**Attestation = Assurance on subject matter**

**Three types:**
1. **Examination** - Highest assurance
2. **Review** - Limited assurance
3. **Agreed-Upon Procedures** - No assurance (just findings)

**Key difference from audit:** Subject matter varies (not just F/S)

sections.title: Examination Engagement
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Express opinion on subject matter or assertion

**Level:** Reasonable assurance (like audit)

**Common subjects:**
• Pro forma financial information
• Prospective financial information
• Compliance with regulations
• Internal control effectiveness
• MD&A
• Service organization controls (SOC 1)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Attestation Types
sections.content:
**"ERA"** of attestation:

**E**xamination = Opinion (high assurance)
**R**eview = Conclusion (limited assurance)
**A**greed-Upon = Findings (no assurance)

**Higher the assurance → More procedures required**

sections.title: Engagement Acceptance
sections.content:
**Requirements to accept:**

• Subject matter is appropriate
• Criteria are suitable and available
• Practitioner has adequate knowledge
• Subject matter can be measured consistently
• Reasonable expectation of conclusion

**Must have suitable criteria!**

sections.title: Suitable Criteria
sections.content:
**Criteria must be:**

• **Objective** - Different practitioners reach same conclusion
• **Measurable** - Reasonably consistent measurement
• **Complete** - All relevant factors included
• **Relevant** - Relevant to subject matter
• **Available** - Users can understand basis

sections.title: Examination Report
sections.content:
**Report elements:**

1. Title including "independent"
2. Identification of subject matter
3. Responsible party's responsibility
4. Practitioner's responsibility
5. Summary of work performed
6. Opinion paragraph
7. Signature, date, location

**Opinion expressed = Reasonable assurance**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Management Assertion
sections.content:
**Two reporting options:**

**1. Opinion on subject matter directly:**
"In our opinion, [the entity] complied with..."

**2. Opinion on management's assertion:**
"In our opinion, management's assertion is fairly stated..."

**Both acceptable—results should be the same!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Examination Engagements: My Take
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Examination engagements can seem like audits' quirky cousins. You're still slinging assurance, but the subject matter? Could be anything! I once had to examine a casino's compliance with their internal controls over complimentary giveaways. Seriously! The point is, it's about providing a high level of assurance, just like an audit, but on something other than financial statements. Don't overthink it. Reasonable assurance is the name of the game, and you need sufficient appropriate evidence to back up your opinion.

sections.title: Mixing Up Examination & Agreed-Upon Procedures
sections.content: Students often confuse examination engagements with agreed-upon procedures because both involve non-financial statement subject matter. The key difference is the level of assurance. In an examination, you're issuing an opinion (reasonable assurance). With agreed-upon procedures, you're just listing the findings, with no opinion or assurance provided. Think of it this way: examination is like saying 'I'm 90% sure this is true,' while agreed-upon procedures is like saying 'Here's what I found, you decide what it means.' Focus on the output - opinion vs. factual findings.

sections.title: Sarah's Pro Forma Predicament
sections.content: Sarah, a senior associate, is reviewing a pro forma financial information examination engagement. The company's management asserts that a recent acquisition will increase revenues by 25% next year. Sarah finds the supporting documentation for this projection weak and based on overly optimistic assumptions. The learning point here is that Sarah needs to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence to support the assertion. If she can't, she'll need to modify her opinion or potentially withdraw from the engagement. Remember, reasonable assurance requires solid evidence!

sections.title: Examination Engagements: Real-World Impact
sections.content: In practice, you'll most likely encounter examination engagements when dealing with specialized industries or specific regulatory requirements. For example, you might examine a hospital's compliance with HIPAA regulations or a software company's adherence to SOC 2 controls. Understanding the attestation standards, especially the examination engagement requirements, is crucial for scoping the engagement, planning the procedures, and ultimately, forming an opinion. Clients rely on your expertise to provide credible assurance on their assertions, improving their reputation and stakeholder confidence.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Attestation provides assurance on various subject matters
Examination = highest level (reasonable assurance/opinion)
Subject matter includes compliance, controls, prospective info
Must have suitable criteria to accept engagement
Can opine on subject matter or management's assertion
AT-C standards govern attestation engagements
Similar rigor to audit but different subject matter

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Attestation Engagements: Which Type is Which?

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following attestation engagements provides the highest level of assurance?
options: Agreed-Upon Procedures Engagement
Review Engagement
Examination Engagement
Compilation Engagement
knowledgeCheck.explanation: An Examination Engagement provides reasonable assurance, which is the highest level of assurance offered in attestation engagements. Agreed-Upon Procedures provide no assurance, and Review Engagements provide limited assurance. Compilation engagements are not attestation engagements.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the level of work the practitioner performs in each type of engagement.

reveal.question: Complete the Sentence: Attestation is...
reveal.answer: ...Assurance on subject matter.

rows.label: Subject Matter
values: Various subjects (e.g., pro forma financial information, prospective financial information, compliance, internal control effectiveness)
Financial Statements

rows.label: Level of Assurance
values: Reasonable Assurance
Reasonable Assurance

rows.label: Purpose
values: Express an opinion on the subject matter or assertion
Express an opinion on whether the financial statements are fairly presented in accordance with applicable framework

title: Attestation: Review Engagements
description: Learn the requirements for attestation review engagements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Review engagements under attestation standards provide LIMITED assurance—less than examination but more than AUP. Understanding what "limited assurance" means and how reviews differ from examinations is key for AUD success!

sections.title: Attestation Review
sections.content:
**Purpose:** Express conclusion on subject matter

**Level:** Limited assurance (moderate)

**Procedures:**
• Inquiry
• Analytical procedures
• Other procedures as necessary

**Less extensive than examination!**

sections.title: Limited vs Reasonable Assurance
sections.content:
**Limited Assurance (Review):**
• "Nothing came to our attention..."
• Negative assurance form
• Meaningful but not high

**Reasonable Assurance (Examination):**
• "In our opinion..."
• Positive assurance form
• High level assurance

**Review = Lower cost, lower assurance**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Review Procedures
sections.content:
**"IAO"** for review procedures:

**I**nquiry (asking questions)
**A**nalytics (comparing data)
**O**ther procedures (as needed)

**No detailed testing like examination!**

**Think:** Review = IAO (I ask only!)

sections.title: Review Report
sections.content:
**Report elements:**

1. Title with "independent"
2. Identification of subject matter
3. Responsibilities (management and practitioner)
4. Nature of review (limited assurance)
5. Conclusion paragraph
6. Signature, date, location

**Conclusion = "Nothing came to our attention..."

sections.title: Review Conclusion Wording
sections.content:
**Standard language:**

"Based on our review, we are not aware of any material modifications that should be made to [subject matter/assertion] in order for it to be in accordance with [criteria]."

**Negative form = Limited assurance**

**NOT: "In our opinion" (that's examination)**

sections.title: When Review is Appropriate
sections.content:
**Consider review when:**

• Lower assurance is acceptable
• Cost is a factor
• Subject matter is less complex
• Risk is moderate

**Examples:**
• Compliance reviews
• Pro forma information
• Sustainability reporting

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Not an Audit Alternative
sections.content:
**Review cannot substitute for audit when:**

• High assurance is required
• Regulatory mandate requires audit
• Users need opinion-level assurance

**Review ≠ Cheaper audit**

**Different purpose, different procedures, different assurance!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Review Engagements: The 'Sniff Test' Audit
sections.content: Alright, let's be honest. A review engagement is like giving a financial statement a really good 'sniff test'. It's not a deep dive like an audit, but more of a 'does anything smell rotten?' kind of thing. You're not guaranteeing anything is perfect, just that nothing obviously jumped out and bit you. Think of it as a quick health checkup versus open-heart surgery. Lower cost for the client, lower risk (and lower fees!) for you. Just remember, 'Nothing came to our attention...' is your mantra.

sections.title: Mixing Up Review and Examination Assurance
sections.content: Students often confuse the level of assurance provided by a review versus an examination. They both involve attestation, but the difference is HUGE. A review offers *limited* assurance, while an examination offers *reasonable* assurance. Think of it this way: 'Limited' means you performed inquiries and analytical procedures, but didn't verify every single transaction. 'Reasonable' means you dug much deeper and performed tests of controls and substantive procedures. Don't let the similar-sounding terms fool you; the work effort and the conclusion are drastically different!

sections.title: Sarah's Spreadsheet Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a senior associate, is performing a review engagement for a small software company. During analytical procedures, she notices a significant increase in marketing expenses compared to the prior year, but the revenue only increased slightly. After inquiry, management explains a new marketing campaign, but provides no supporting documentation. Sarah needs to document this inconsistency and consider the implications for her 'nothing came to our attention' conclusion, as further investigation might be needed to support management's explanation.

sections.title: Review Engagements: A Startup's Best Friend
sections.content: In practice, you'll frequently encounter review engagements when working with startups or smaller businesses. They often need some level of assurance for investors or lenders, but can't justify the cost of a full audit. A review provides a cost-effective way to achieve that. Understanding the scope and limitations of a review is crucial because clients may initially underestimate the work involved, or overestimate the level of assurance it provides. Setting realistic expectations upfront is key to a successful engagement.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Review provides limited (negative) assurance
Procedures: Inquiry, analytics, other procedures
Less extensive than examination
Conclusion: "Nothing came to our attention..."
Lower cost but also lower assurance
Cannot substitute when high assurance needed
Appropriate when moderate assurance is acceptable

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is most accurate regarding the level of assurance provided in a review engagement?
options: A review engagement provides reasonable assurance that the subject matter is free from material misstatement.
A review engagement provides absolute assurance that the subject matter is fairly stated.
A review engagement provides limited assurance, expressed in the form of negative assurance, regarding the subject matter.
A review engagement provides no assurance regarding the subject matter.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A review engagement provides limited assurance, expressed in the form of negative assurance (e.g., 'Nothing came to our attention'). This is a lower level of assurance than the reasonable assurance provided in an examination engagement.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the phrase used in the conclusion paragraph of a review report.

reveal.question: What is the standard phrase used in the conclusion paragraph of a review report to express negative assurance?
reveal.answer: "Nothing came to our attention that would indicate that the subject matter is not presented in accordance with [the applicable criteria]."

rows.label: Level of Assurance
values: Reasonable (High)
Limited (Moderate)

rows.label: Form of Assurance
values: Positive (In our opinion...)
Negative (Nothing came to our attention...)

rows.label: Procedures Performed
values: More extensive procedures, including testing and verification
Primarily inquiry and analytical procedures

rows.label: Cost
values: Higher
Lower

title: Agreed-Upon Procedures (AUP)
description: Master agreed-upon procedures engagements

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: AUP engagements are unique—no opinion, no conclusion, just findings! Users engage the practitioner to perform specific procedures and report what was found. Understanding this "procedures only" engagement is essential for AUD!

sections.title: What is AUP?
sections.content:
**Agreed-Upon Procedures:**

• Practitioner performs only agreed procedures
• Reports only factual findings
• NO opinion or conclusion
• Users draw their own conclusions

**Think:** "Here's what I did, here's what I found. You decide what it means."

sections.title: Characteristics of AUP
sections.content:
**Key features:**

• Procedures must be agreed upon with engaging party
• Procedures must be sufficient for intended use
• Report lists procedures performed
• Report presents factual findings only
• No assurance provided
• Users must be able to evaluate findings

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: AUP Elements
sections.content:
**"FACT"** describes AUP:

**F**indings only (no opinion)
**A**greed procedures (specified by users)
**C**ustomized engagement
**T**hey conclude (users, not practitioner)

**Just the FACTs, ma'am!**

sections.title: Engagement Acceptance
sections.content:
**Requirements:**

• Procedures must be agreed upon
• Engaging party acknowledges appropriateness
• Measurable subject matter
• Findings can be objectively determined
• Limited to those who agreed to procedures

**Prior restriction on use was removed in 2020!**

sections.title: AUP Report Contents
sections.content:
**Report includes:**

1. Title
2. Subject matter identification
3. Responsible party and practitioner responsibilities
4. Procedures performed (in detail)
5. Findings for each procedure
6. Statement: No opinion or conclusion expressed
7. Signature, date, location

**Very detailed on procedures!**

sections.title: Sample Procedures and Findings
sections.content:
**Procedure:** Compare aged accounts receivable trial balance to general ledger.

**Finding:** The aged trial balance total of $1,456,789 agreed to the general ledger balance.

**Procedure:** Select 25 largest customer balances and mail confirmations.

**Finding:** 23 of 25 confirmations returned. 22 agreed; 1 showed $1,200 timing difference due to payment in transit.

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: No Assurance
sections.content:
**AUP provides NO assurance!**

**Cannot state:**
• "In our opinion..."
• "Based on our review..."
• "We conclude..."

**Must state:**
"We make no representation regarding the sufficiency of the procedures."

**Users must evaluate adequacy of procedures and findings!**

sections.title: Common AUP Applications
sections.content:
**Frequently used for:**

• Royalty audits
• Grant compliance
• Debt covenant verification
• Real estate closing statements
• Insurance claims
• Due diligence support
• Specific account testing

**Flexible—can cover anything with measurable subject matter**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: AUPs: The 'Just the Facts, Ma'am' Gig
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. AUPs are like being a super-detailed observer. You're not there to judge, just to report. Think of it as CSI: Accounting. You find the evidence, lay it out, and let the client (or the judge, in CSI terms) draw their own conclusions. No opinions from you! Just the cold, hard facts. It might feel weird at first, but it's actually kinda liberating. Plus, less liability if you stick to the agreed-upon procedures and factual findings. Seriously, document everything!

sections.title: AUP vs. Limited Assurance: Don't Mix 'Em!
sections.content: Students often confuse AUP engagements with limited assurance engagements (reviews) because both provide a lower level of assurance than a full audit. However, the key difference is that an AUP engagement provides NO assurance. You're just listing procedures and findings. A review, on the other hand, provides limited assurance, expressed as 'nothing came to our attention that...'. Avoid this mistake by remembering the core principle of AUP: no assurance, just facts. Understand the engagement letter and stick to the agreed procedures.

sections.title: Bob's Inventory Count Conundrum
sections.content: Bob, a senior associate, was assigned an AUP engagement to verify a client's inventory count. The client specifically requested counting only items in Warehouse A. Bob, noticing Warehouse B was overflowing, felt Warehouse A was unrepresentative. He added procedures to count a sample in Warehouse B. The problem? Bob went beyond the agreed-upon procedures. He exposed the firm to liability and may have breached the contract. Stick to the script!

sections.title: AUPs in the Real World: Due Diligence
sections.content: In practice, you'll frequently encounter AUPs during mergers and acquisitions. For example, a company might engage you to verify the revenue stream of a target company for the past three years, focusing specifically on related party transactions. Your report will detail the procedures performed (e.g., tracing invoices to bank statements, confirming sales with customers) and the factual findings (e.g., $1 million in sales to a company owned by the CEO's brother). The acquiring company then uses your findings to assess the risk of the acquisition. Master AUPs now, and you'll be a due diligence rockstar!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AUP = Agreed procedures performed, findings reported
NO opinion or conclusion—factual findings only
Procedures must be agreed upon with engaging party
Users draw their own conclusions from findings
Report details each procedure and its finding
Use restriction removed in 2020 (broader distribution OK)
Common for royalties, grants, covenants, due diligence

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is most accurate regarding an agreed-upon procedures (AUP) engagement?
options: The practitioner expresses an opinion on the subject matter.
The report is restricted to the parties who agreed to the procedures.
The practitioner provides limited assurance on the subject matter.
The practitioner reports only factual findings based on the agreed-upon procedures.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is that the practitioner reports only factual findings. In an AUP engagement, the practitioner performs specific procedures agreed upon with the engaging party and reports only the results of those procedures. No opinion or assurance is provided.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the core difference between an AUP and other attestation engagements.

reveal.question: What are the three key requirements for accepting an Agreed-Upon Procedures (AUP) engagement?
reveal.answer:
1. Procedures must be agreed upon.
2. Engaging party acknowledges appropriateness of procedures.
3. Measurable subject matter; findings can be objectively determined. (Limited to those who agreed to procedures.)

knowledgeCheck.question: A practitioner is engaged to perform an agreed-upon procedures engagement. Which of the following is NOT a necessary condition for acceptance of the engagement?
options: The procedures to be performed are agreed to by the practitioner and the engaging party.
The subject matter is measurable and objective.
The engaging party takes responsibility for the sufficiency of the procedures.
The practitioner expresses an opinion on the fairness of the subject matter.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The correct answer is that the practitioner expresses an opinion on the fairness of the subject matter. AUP engagements specifically exclude the expression of an opinion or any form of assurance. The engaging party acknowledges the appropriateness of the procedures, and the subject matter must be measurable and objective.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Recall what the practitioner *cannot* do in an AUP engagement.

title: SSARS: Compilation Engagements
description: Understand compilation services under SSARS

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Compilations are the most common accounting service for small businesses! Under SSARS, CPAs assist in preparing financial statements but provide NO assurance. Understanding what's required—and what's NOT—is essential for serving private company clients!

sections.title: What is Compilation?
sections.content:
**Compilation:**

• CPA assists in presenting F/S
• Uses management's information
• NO assurance expressed
• Reading for obvious errors only
• Management takes responsibility

**Governed by:** SSARS (Statements on Standards for Accounting and Review Services)

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Compilation
sections.content:
**"PRESENT"** the compilation:

**P**repare F/S from management info
**R**ead for obvious errors
**E**ngagement letter required
**S**SARS governs
**E**xpress NO assurance
**N**o independence required
**T**ake care—still responsible!

**Compilation = PRESENT, don't verify!**

sections.title: Required Procedures
sections.content:
**What accountant MUST do:**

• Obtain engagement letter
• Have knowledge of industry
• Understand client's accounting policies
• Read F/S for obvious material errors
• Ensure appropriate framework

**What accountant does NOT do:**
• Verify information
• Test underlying data
• Obtain evidence

sections.title: Independence NOT Required
sections.content:
**Unique to compilation:**

• Independence NOT required
• BUT—if not independent, must disclose in report
• Reason for impairment not required to be stated

**Example:** "We are not independent with respect to XYZ Company."

**Still must be competent and ethical!**

sections.title: Compilation Report
sections.content:
**Report elements:**

1. Title (no "independent" if not independent)
2. Addressee
3. Identification of F/S compiled
4. Management's responsibility statement
5. Accountant's responsibility statement
6. Statement that NO assurance expressed
7. Signature, date, city

**Key statement:** "We have not audited or reviewed..."

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: GAAP Departures
sections.content:
**If F/S depart from GAAP:**

**Option 1:** Modify report
• Disclose departure in separate paragraph
• May include dollar effect if known

**Option 2:** Withdrawal
• If client won't correct material departures
• Consider if association is appropriate

**Can compile with GAAP departures—just disclose!**

sections.title: Omission of Disclosures
sections.content:
**Substantially all disclosures may be omitted IF:**

• Not intended to mislead users
• Report indicates omission

**Report states:** "Management has elected to omit substantially all disclosures required by [framework]."

**Cannot selectively omit—all or none!**
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Compilation: The 'Just Showing' Service
sections.content: Okay, so compilations. Think of it like this: your client hands you a pile of spreadsheets and says, 'Make this look like a real financial statement, please!' You're basically formatting their data. You're NOT verifying anything. No assurance, zip, nada. Your job is to read through it and ask, 'Hey, does this seem totally bonkers?' If it does, you point it out. If not, you compile. Just remember, you're the stylist, not the judge. Don't get caught up playing detective when you're just supposed to be making it pretty.

sections.title: Compilation vs. Review: Assurance Confusion
sections.content: Students often confuse compilations with reviews, especially when memorizing the levels of assurance. The biggest mistake is thinking a compilation provides some level of assurance, even minimal. It DOESN'T. A review provides limited assurance, while a compilation provides none. Remember, in a compilation, you're primarily reading for obvious errors; you're not performing analytical procedures or inquiries like you would in a review. If the question mentions 'analytical procedures' or 'inquiries,' it's likely a review, not a compilation.

sections.title: Sarah's Spreadsheet Surprise
sections.content: Sarah is compiling financial statements for a local bakery. While reading the draft, she notices a large negative balance in the 'Cash' account. It's clearly an overdraft, but it's presented as a regular cash balance. Sarah must bring this to management's attention, because that's an obvious material error. She can't just ignore it, even though it's 'just a compilation.' If management refuses to correct it, Sarah needs to consider disclosing the departure from GAAP in her report or even withdrawing from the engagement.

sections.title: Compilations: The Small Business Lifeline
sections.content: In practice, you'll likely perform compilations for small businesses that need basic financial statements for loan applications or internal management. These clients often can't afford the cost of a full audit or review. Understanding the SSARS requirements for compilations is crucial because you'll use them frequently. You'll need a solid engagement letter outlining the scope of the service and clearly stating that no assurance is provided. Get comfortable with the compilation report template; you'll be using it a lot!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Compilation = Assist in presenting F/S, NO assurance
Independence NOT required (but disclose if not independent)
SSARS governs compilation engagements
Read for obvious errors only—no verification
GAAP departures: Disclose or withdraw
Substantially all disclosures may be omitted if disclosed
Engagement letter required

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following procedures is *not* required in a compilation engagement performed in accordance with SSARS?
options: Obtaining an engagement letter documenting the scope of the services to be performed.
Reading the financial statements to identify obvious material errors.
Understanding the client's industry and the accounting principles and practices generally used in the industry.
Assessing the client's internal control over financial reporting.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Assessing internal control over financial reporting is not a required procedure in a compilation engagement. A compilation provides no assurance, and the accountant is not required to verify information or test underlying data.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the level of assurance provided in a compilation engagement.

sections.title: Compilation Engagement: What MUST an accountant do?

reveal.question: List three procedures an accountant *must* perform in a compilation engagement.
reveal.answer:
1. Obtain an engagement letter.
2. Possess knowledge of the industry.
3. Understand the client's accounting policies.
4. Read the financial statements for obvious material errors.
5. Ensure the appropriate financial reporting framework is used.

knowledgeCheck.question: An accountant is performing a compilation engagement. During the engagement, they discover a potential misstatement that management is unwilling to correct. What is the accountant's most appropriate course of action?
options: Withdraw from the engagement.
Issue a qualified compilation report, disclosing the misstatement.
Issue an adverse compilation report.
Accept management's decision and continue with the compilation, as no assurance is provided.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: If management refuses to correct a material misstatement, the accountant should withdraw from the compilation engagement. While a compilation provides no assurance, the accountant cannot knowingly be associated with materially misstated financial statements.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the accountant's responsibility to not be associated with misleading information.

title: SSARS: Review Engagements
description: Master review services for non-public entities

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Reviews under SSARS provide limited assurance on private company financial statements—more than compilation but less than audit! Understanding the procedures and report language is essential. Reviews are very common in practice and on the exam!

sections.title: SSARS Review
sections.content:
**Review engagement:**

• Provides LIMITED assurance
• Based primarily on inquiry and analytics
• Less scope than audit
• Independence IS required
• Governed by SSARS (AR-C 90)

**Conclusion:** "We are not aware of any material modifications..."

sections.title: Review Procedures
sections.content:
**Primary procedures:**

**1. Inquiry:**
• Of management about accounting practices
• About unusual transactions
• About subsequent events

**2. Analytical procedures:**
• Compare to prior periods
• Compare to expectations
• Investigate unusual fluctuations

**Not designed to obtain reasonable assurance!**

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Review = IA
sections.content:
**Review = "I Ask"**

**I** = Inquiry (asking questions)
**A** = Analytics (comparing numbers)

**That's primarily it!**

**No detailed testing, sampling, or confirmation**

**Just ASK and ANALYZE**

sections.title: Independence Requirement
sections.content:
**Must be independent for review!**

**If not independent:**
• Cannot perform review
• Must compile instead (or decline)

**This is different from compilation** where independence not required

**No disclosure option—simply cannot do review without independence**

sections.title: Review Report
sections.content:
**Report elements:**

1. Title including "Independent"
2. Addressee
3. Identification of F/S reviewed
4. Management's responsibility
5. Accountant's responsibility
6. Description of review (limited assurance)
7. Conclusion paragraph
8. Signature, date, city

sections.title: Review Conclusion Wording
sections.content:
**Standard conclusion:**

"Based on our review, we are not aware of any material modifications that should be made to the accompanying financial statements in order for them to be in accordance with accounting principles generally accepted in the United States of America."

**Negative form = Limited assurance**

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Review vs Audit Procedures
sections.content:
**Review does NOT include:**

• Confirmation of receivables
• Physical inventory observation
• Testing internal controls
• Sampling transactions
• Obtaining third-party evidence

**If you find yourself doing these → You're auditing, not reviewing!**

**Review = Inquiry + Analytics only**

sections.title: Known Departures from GAAP
sections.content:
**If material GAAP departure found:**

**Option 1:** Request management to revise

**Option 2:** Modify report
• Describe departure
• Include effect if known
• Qualified or adverse conclusion

**Option 3:** Withdraw
• If modification inadequate
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Review Engagements: The 'Sniff Test' Audit
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. A review engagement is NOT an audit. Think of it like this: an audit is a deep dive, whereas a review is more like a 'sniff test'. You're asking questions, doing some quick analytics, and basically trying to see if anything smells rotten. You're providing limited assurance, so don't go overboard. SSARS is your bible here (AR-C 90), and remember, independence is key. If something feels off, dig a little deeper, but don't expect to find every single skeleton in the closet. Your conclusion? 'We are not aware of any material modifications...'

sections.title: Confusing Review & Compilation: Assurance Levels
sections.content: Students often confuse review engagements with compilation engagements. The big difference? Assurance! A review provides *limited* assurance, based on inquiry and analytical procedures. A compilation, on the other hand, provides *no* assurance. It's basically taking management's data and putting it into financial statement format. Students forget that a review requires procedures beyond just compiling the data. Always remember the assurance levels and the associated procedures. If you're performing inquiries and analytics, you're likely in review territory, not compilation.

sections.title: Sarah's Unusual Revenue Spike
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, noticed a 30% increase in revenue for Q3 compared to the prior year during a review engagement. She immediately focused on inquiry, asking management about any significant new contracts or market changes. Management explained that a large, one-time project caused the revenue spike. The learning point is that unusual fluctuations identified through analytical procedures should always prompt further inquiry to understand the underlying reasons, even if they seem plausible on the surface.

sections.title: Spotting Red Flags in Review: Real-World Application
sections.content: In practice, you'll often use review engagements for smaller, privately held companies that don't need a full audit. A common scenario is reviewing the financials of a family-owned business applying for a loan. You'll be looking for red flags like inconsistent accounting practices, unusually high debt levels, or significant related-party transactions. Mastering inquiry and analytical procedures now will help you quickly identify potential problems and provide valuable insights to both the company and the lender. Don't underestimate the power of a well-executed review – it can be a lifesaver!

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Review provides LIMITED assurance (negative form)
Procedures: Primarily inquiry and analytics
Independence IS required for reviews
No detailed testing, sampling, or confirmations
Conclusion: "Not aware of any material modifications..."
GAAP departures: Modify report or withdraw
More assurance than compilation, less than audit

sections.content: <!-- AUTO-GENERATED INTERACTIVE SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding a review engagement performed under SSARS?
options: A review engagement provides reasonable assurance that no material modifications should be made to the financial statements in order for them to be in accordance with the applicable financial reporting framework.
Independence is not required for a review engagement, as long as the lack of independence is disclosed in the review report.
The primary procedures performed in a review engagement are designed to obtain reasonable assurance that the financial statements are free of material misstatement.

A review engagement provides limited assurance that there are no material modifications that should be made to the financial statements for them to be in accordance with the applicable financial reporting framework.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A review engagement provides limited assurance, not reasonable assurance. Independence is required. The procedures are not designed to obtain reasonable assurance.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the level of assurance provided in a review engagement compared to an audit.

reveal.question: What are the TWO primary procedures performed in a review engagement?
reveal.answer: Inquiry and Analytical Procedures

rows.label: Assurance Level
values: Limited Assurance
No Assurance

rows.label: Independence Required
values: Yes
No

rows.label: Governing Standards
values: SSARS (AR-C 90)
SSARS (AR-C 80)

rows.label: Report Conclusion
values: 'We are not aware of any material modifications...'
No opinion or assurance expressed

title: SSARS: Preparation Engagements
description: Understand the preparation of financial statements service

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Preparation engagements are the simplest SSARS service! The CPA prepares financial statements but issues NO report. Understanding this "report-less" service and when it's appropriate is important for serving small business clients!

sections.title: What is Preparation?
sections.content:
**Preparation engagement:**

• CPA prepares F/S for management
• NO report is issued
• NO assurance provided
• Management uses for internal purposes

**Simplest SSARS engagement—no report!**

**Governed by:** AR-C 70

sections.title: 🧠 Memory Aid: Preparation
sections.content:
**"NO Report, LEGEND"**

**NO R**eport is issued
**LEGEND** on each page

**That's the key difference from compilation!**

Preparation = Legend instead of report

sections.title: Legend Requirement
sections.content:
**Each page of F/S must include legend:**

**"No assurance is provided on these financial statements"**

**Or similar wording that:**
• Indicates no assurance
• Identifies the CPA (optional)

**Purpose:** Users know no assurance was provided

sections.title: When to Use Preparation
sections.content:
**Appropriate when:**

• F/S for internal use only
• No external users need assurance
• Management just needs F/S prepared
• Cost is a primary factor

**Not appropriate when:**
• Third parties will rely on F/S
• Bank or creditor requires compilation/review
• Assurance is needed

sections.title: ⚠️ Exam Trap: Third-Party Use
sections.content:
**CPA should assess intended use:**

**If F/S will go to third parties:**
• Consider compilation or review instead
• Preparation may not be appropriate
• Bank/creditor may require report

**Preparation is really for INTERNAL use only**

**No prohibition, but consider appropriateness!**

sections.title: Required Documentation
sections.content:
**Must document:**

• Engagement letter (signed)
• Understanding of terms
• Legend requirement agreement

**Engagement letter specifies:**
• Services to be performed
• No report will be issued
• Legend will be included
• Management responsibilities
<!-- ENGAGEMENT SECTIONS (2026-02-28) -->

sections.title: Preparation Engagements: The 'No-Assurance' Zone
sections.content: Alright, let's be real. Preparation engagements? They're like the vanilla ice cream of SSARS. Super basic. You're essentially taking management's numbers and putting them into financial statement format. No opinion, no review, just straight-up preparation. Think of it as glorified bookkeeping with a CPA's signature (sort of!). The key is that big, bold disclaimer: "No assurance provided." Don't forget it. Seriously, it's the only thing protecting you in this engagement. Get that legend on every page, or you'll be singing a different tune when things go south.

sections.title: Confusing Preparation with Compilation Engagements
sections.content: Students often confuse preparation engagements with compilation engagements because both are relatively low-level services. The crucial difference is the report. Preparation engagements require NO report, just the disclaimer on each page of the financial statements. Compilation engagements, on the other hand, REQUIRE a report, albeit a basic one stating that no assurance is provided. Remember: If a report is issued, it's a compilation. If no report, just a disclaimer on the F/S themselves, it's a preparation engagement. Focus on the report to easily differentiate the two.

sections.title: Sarah's Simple SSARS Snafu
sections.content: Sarah, a staff accountant, prepared financial statements for a small landscaping company as a side gig. She formatted the income statement and balance sheet perfectly, but forgot to include the 'No assurance' disclaimer on each page. When the company later faced financial difficulties and the bank questioned the F/S, Sarah was potentially liable because the statements implied a level of assurance that wasn't provided. Moral of the story: Always, ALWAYS include the 'No assurance' legend on every page of prepared financial statements.

sections.title: Real-World Relevance: Preparation Engagements
sections.content: In practice, you'll frequently encounter preparation engagements when assisting small businesses or startups that need basic financial statements for internal management or tax purposes, but don't require the cost or level of assurance of a compilation or review. Think sole proprietorships or small partnerships. It's a quick and efficient way to provide value without taking on significant risk. Knowing the rules inside and out will allow you to offer this service confidently, knowing you're compliant with SSARS and protecting yourself from potential liability. It's bread and butter for many small firms.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
